# INTERNAL MEMO

**TO:** All Employees
**FROM:** Dana Reeves, VP of Engineering
**DATE:** June 12, 2025
**RE:** Summary and Follow-Up — System Outage on June 10–11

---

Team,

I want to give everyone a clear and honest account of the system outage that disrupted operations on Tuesday and into early Wednesday morning. I know the past 48 hours have been frustrating, and you deserve a full explanation.

## What Happened

On Tuesday, June 10, at approximately 10:15 AM ET, a routine database migration triggered an unexpected cascade failure across our primary authentication servers. This caused employees and customers to lose access to Relay (our internal project platform), the customer-facing portal, and all integrated tools that depend on single sign-on, including email, file storage, and the support ticketing system.

The root cause was a configuration conflict between the updated database schema and a legacy authentication module that had not been flagged during pre-migration testing. When the new schema went live, the authentication service entered a loop of failed validation checks that overwhelmed the server cluster and brought down dependent systems one by one.

## What We Fixed

Our infrastructure and platform teams worked through the night to resolve the issue. Here is what was done:

- **Tuesday, 3:40 PM ET** — We isolated the affected authentication module and restored email and file storage access on a backup pathway.
- **Tuesday, 9:15 PM ET** — We completed a rollback of the database migration and confirmed data integrity across all production tables. No customer or employee data was lost or compromised.
- **Wednesday, 1:50 AM ET** — Full service was restored across all systems, including the customer portal and Relay.
- **Wednesday morning** — We ran a battery of integration tests and confirmed stable operation across all environments.

## What You Should Expect Next

**Over the coming days and weeks, we are taking several steps:**

1. **System monitoring will be elevated.** Our operations team is running enhanced monitoring through the end of the month. If you experience any lingering slowness, login issues, or missing data, report them immediately to IT Support at helpdesk@internal or ext. 4100.

2. **Some scheduled maintenance will be postponed.** We are pushing back non-critical platform updates originally planned for June 18 until we complete our review process. You will receive updated timelines by the end of next week.

3. **A full post-incident review is underway.** Engineering is conducting a thorough review that will include an audit of our migration testing procedures, our legacy dependency map, and our failover protocols. We will share a summary of findings and planned improvements with department leads by June 27.

4. **Customers have been notified.** Our communications team sent a notice to affected customers yesterday afternoon. If clients reach out to you directly with questions, please route them to your account manager or to support@company.com.

## A Note of Thanks

I want to recognize the infrastructure, platform, and IT support teams who worked around the clock to bring our systems back online. I also want to thank every one of you for your patience and for finding ways to keep work moving during the disruption. Several teams coordinated workarounds on their own within minutes, and that resourcefulness made a real difference.

We hold ourselves to a high standard of reliability, and we fell short of it this week. The steps we are taking now are designed to make sure this specific failure cannot happen again and to strengthen our systems against similar risks in the future.

If you have questions or concerns, my door is open. You can also reach me directly at d.reeves@internal.

Thank you,

**Dana Reeves**
VP of Engineering

**INTERNAL MEMO**

**To:** All Employees
**From:** Leadership Team
**Date:** June 12, 2025
**Re:** New Flexible Remote Work Policy — Effective August 1, 2025

---

**Purpose of This Memo**

We are writing to announce a significant update to how and where we work. After several months of planning, employee feedback sessions, and operational review, we are introducing a new flexible remote work policy that will take effect on August 1, 2025. Please read this memo carefully, as it affects every member of our team.

---

**The Policy**

Starting August 1, all employees may work remotely up to four days per week. Each team will designate one shared in-office day per week, coordinated by its manager. On that day, team members are expected to be present at headquarters for collaborative work, planning sessions, and face-to-face connection.

Here are the key details:

- **Remote eligibility is company-wide.** Every department and role is included. Where job duties require occasional additional on-site presence (for example, hardware testing or facility-dependent work), managers will communicate those expectations directly.
- **Core collaboration hours are 10:00 AM–3:00 PM Eastern, Monday through Friday.** Regardless of where you work, you should be available and responsive during this window. Beyond those hours, you have flexibility to structure your day in the way that works best for you and your team.
- **Your designated in-office day is mandatory, not optional.** This is the backbone of the policy. We are preserving in-person collaboration, not eliminating it. Managers will finalize team schedules by July 15 and communicate them directly.
- **The office remains open every weekday.** You are welcome to come in more often than the required one day. Desks will shift to a hot-desk model, with reservations managed through a booking system we will roll out in July.

---

**Why We Are Doing This**

Three things drove this decision.

**First, our own data supports it.** Over the past year, we piloted remote-flexible arrangements with three teams. Productivity held steady or improved in every case. Sprint velocity, deployment frequency, and customer response times were unaffected. Meanwhile, those teams reported measurably higher satisfaction scores in our Q1 engagement survey.

**Second, you asked for it.** In our April employee survey, 81% of respondents said flexible remote work was a top factor in their day-to-day job satisfaction, and 67% said it directly influenced whether they planned to stay with the company long-term. We heard that clearly.

**Third, it makes us more competitive.** We are a 120-person company competing for talent against organizations of every size. A thoughtful remote work policy expands our ability to hire excellent people regardless of geography and helps us retain the excellent people we already have.

We are not making this change because in-person work has no value — it clearly does. We are making it because rigid five-day office requirements no longer reflect how our teams actually do their best work.

---

**What You Need to Do Next**

Please complete the following steps before July 15:

1. **Read the full policy document.** It will be posted on the company intranet by June 18 and covers equipment stipends, expense guidelines, information security requirements for remote work, and other details not summarized here.

2. **Confirm your remote work setup.** You will receive a short Remote Workspace Checklist from IT by June 20. Complete and return it by July 1. This helps us ensure everyone has reliable connectivity, appropriate equipment, and a workspace that meets our security standards.

3. **Talk with your manager.** Each manager will hold a team meeting before July 15 to confirm your team's designated in-office day, discuss how async communication will work, and address questions specific to your group.

4. **Attend one of two company-wide Q&A sessions.** We will hold live sessions on **June 25 at 11:00 AM ET** and **June 27 at 2:00 PM ET** via video call. Leadership will walk through the policy, answer questions, and hear your concerns. Calendar invitations will follow this week.

---

**A Final Note**

This policy is not permanent in the sense that it is beyond evaluation. We will formally review it after six months — looking at the same performance metrics, collaboration quality, and employee feedback that informed this decision in the first place. If something isn't working, we will adjust.

We believe this is the right step for our company and our people. If you have immediate questions before the Q&A sessions, please reach out to your manager or to HR at remotepolicy@[company].com.

Thank you for your continued commitment to building great software — wherever you happen to be sitting when you do it.

---

**Leadership Team**

# It's Time to Give Buses Their Own Lane

**The math is simple. The politics shouldn't be this hard.**

Every weekday morning, the same scene plays out on our city's busiest corridors. Thousands of commuters sit alone in their cars, inching forward, watching the minutes tick away. And right there beside them, stuck in the same gridlock, sits a 40-foot bus carrying fifty people — people who chose the option we told them was better for the city, only to be punished with the same crawling commute as everyone else.

This is a policy failure, and there is a straightforward fix: dedicated bus lanes.

I know. The moment anyone suggests removing a lane of general traffic, a predictable uproar follows. Business owners worry about lost customers. Drivers worry about worse congestion. Politicians worry about angry constituents. These concerns deserve a serious response — and they have one. The evidence from cities around the world overwhelmingly shows that dedicated bus lanes work, and that the fears surrounding them are largely unfounded.

## The Efficiency Argument Is Unanswerable

Consider a single lane of urban roadway during peak hours. Filled with private automobiles averaging 1.2 occupants each, that lane moves roughly 600 to 1,600 people per hour. Convert it to a dedicated bus lane, and the same strip of asphalt can move 4,000 to 8,000 people per hour. That isn't a marginal improvement. It is a transformation in how efficiently we use our most constrained public resource: road space.

When a bus carrying fifty passengers sits behind twenty single-occupancy vehicles at a red light, we are asking fifty people to wait for twenty. That is not a neutral allocation of space. It is a choice — a choice to prioritize the storage of private metal over the movement of human beings.

## Speed Attracts Riders, and Riders Reduce Congestion

The single greatest predictor of whether someone will ride a bus is whether that bus is fast and reliable. Dedicated lanes deliver both improvements immediately. Cities that have implemented bus rapid transit with dedicated lanes routinely see travel times drop by 20 to 35 percent and ridership increase by 30 percent or more. Indianapolis saw a 130 percent ridership jump on its Red Line. Cleveland's HealthLine corridor saw $9.5 billion in economic development along its route.

More riders on buses means fewer cars on the road — including on the remaining general-traffic lanes. This is not theoretical. After New York City installed a dedicated bus lane on First and Second Avenues in Manhattan, overall corridor travel times improved for *all* vehicles, not just buses. The same pattern emerged in London, in Bogotá, in Seoul. When you give people a genuinely competitive transit option, enough drivers switch to decompress the entire system.

## "But What About the Businesses?"

This is the objection that carries the most emotional weight, and it deserves honest engagement. Small business owners on a commercial corridor are right to worry about disruption during construction. They are right to want customers to be able to reach them.

But the data tells a consistent story: bus lanes are good for business. A study of bus rapid transit corridors in multiple U.S. cities found that retail sales along those corridors grew faster than citywide averages after implementation. The reason is straightforward. A bus lane doesn't eliminate access to a street; it reorganizes it. And a reliable, high-frequency bus route delivers a steady stream of pedestrians — actual foot traffic — directly to storefronts in a way that pass-through car traffic never does. A driver sealed in a vehicle at 30 miles per hour is not a customer. A person stepping off a bus at the corner is.

## Equity Demands It

There is another dimension to this debate that too often goes unspoken. The people riding buses in most American cities are disproportionately lower-income, disproportionately people of color, and disproportionately essential workers — the nurses, custodians, cooks, and caregivers who keep the city running. When we refuse to dedicate lane space to transit, we are effectively telling the people with the fewest transportation choices that their time is worth less than the time of those who can afford to drive.

A bus stuck in traffic doesn't just mean a longer commute. It means a parent arriving late to pick up a child from daycare and paying a penalty fee she can't afford. It means a home health aide losing an hour of wages. It means a worker who depends on a transfer watching his connecting bus pull away because the first one was fifteen minutes late.

Dedicated bus lanes are one of the most direct, cost-effective investments a city can make in transportation equity. They require no new technology, no decade-long construction timeline, and no billion-dollar bond measure. They require paint, signs, enforcement, and political courage.

## The Real Question

The debate over bus lanes is often framed as cars versus buses, drivers versus riders. But that framing is false. The real question is simpler: **How do we move the most people, most efficiently, through limited space?**

Every traffic engineer knows the answer. Every city that has tried dedicated bus lanes has confirmed it. The only thing standing in the way is the persistent, deeply American illusion that a lane of traffic "belongs" to cars — that the natural state of a public road is one filled with private vehicles, and that any reallocation is a taking rather than a correction.

Our congested corridors are already failing. They are failing drivers, who sit in traffic. They are failing bus riders, who sit in worse traffic. They are failing businesses, which struggle with access. They are failing the climate, as idling engines pour carbon into the atmosphere block after block.

A dedicated bus lane won't solve every urban transportation challenge. But it is the single highest-impact, lowest-cost intervention available to a city that is serious about moving people. The paint is ready. The evidence is in.

All that's missing is the will.

# Bring Back the Corner Store: Why Our Neighborhood Needs Small Shops on Residential Streets

**By a concerned resident who's tired of driving ten minutes for a carton of eggs**

There was a time in this country when nearly every neighborhood had a corner store — a small, unassuming shop wedged between houses where you could grab a loaf of bread, a gallon of milk, or a bag of ice without buckling a seatbelt. Those stores didn't destroy neighborhoods. They *were* the neighborhood. And it's time we brought them back.

Our current zoning laws treat residential areas like sealed containers, strictly separated from even the most modest commercial activity. The result? Streets that empty out during the day, neighbors who never cross paths, and a fifteen-minute drive every time you realize you're out of butter. This rigid separation was supposed to protect our quality of life. Instead, it has quietly hollowed it out.

## The Case Is Simpler Than You Think

Let me be clear about what I'm proposing. I'm not talking about big-box retail, drive-throughs, or neon-lit strip malls. I'm talking about small, neighborhood-scaled shops — under 1,500 square feet — operating during reasonable hours, owned by people who likely live within walking distance. A place with a front door, a register, and maybe a rack of local produce.

The objections are predictable: traffic, noise, property values, "character of the neighborhood." I understand the instinct to protect what we have. But let's examine these concerns honestly, because the evidence tells a very different story.

**Traffic.** Corner stores, by their very nature, serve the people who live around them. They generate foot traffic, not car traffic. A well-placed neighborhood shop actually *reduces* the number of vehicle trips residents take, because it eliminates the need to drive across town for a forgotten ingredient or a morning coffee. Fewer cars on arterial roads, fewer emissions, less congestion — that's not a problem, it's a solution.

**Noise.** A small store with a handful of customers at any given time is quieter than the average backyard barbecue. It's quieter than the landscaping crews that roar through every Thursday. It's certainly quieter than the delivery trucks already rolling through the neighborhood because online shopping has turned every house on the block into a mini-warehouse.

**Property values.** This is the concern that gets the most emotional energy and the least factual scrutiny. Study after study — from urban planning researchers at institutions like the University of Virginia, MIT, and the Brookings Institution — has shown that walkable access to neighborhood retail *increases* property values. Homes near small-scale commercial amenities consistently sell for more than comparable homes in car-dependent residential-only zones. Walkability is one of the most sought-after features in today's real estate market, and it's nearly impossible to achieve without mixed-use zoning.

## What We've Lost

Strict single-use zoning is a relatively modern invention. For most of American history, the corner store was standard. It wasn't just a place to buy things — it was the connective tissue of community life. It was where you ran into your neighbors, where the elderly couple down the street could walk to get what they needed without depending on someone to drive them, where teenagers got their first jobs, where a local entrepreneur could start a business without taking on a commercial lease worth tens of thousands of dollars.

When we eliminated the corner store, we didn't just lose convenience. We lost a hundred casual daily interactions that used to hold communities together. We replaced them with car trips to big retailers where we don't know anyone and no one knows us. We traded the living neighborhood for the quiet, empty, aesthetically controlled residential zone — and then we wondered why we felt so isolated.

The consequences fall hardest on those who can least afford them. Elderly residents who no longer drive. Low-income families with one car that's at work all day. Teenagers too young for a license. People with disabilities. For these neighbors, the absence of a walkable store isn't an inconvenience — it's a daily hardship that limits their independence and their access to fresh food.

## This Already Works — Everywhere

We don't have to speculate about whether neighborhood-integrated retail works. We can simply look at the places people love most.

Charleston. Savannah. The older neighborhoods of Portland, Minneapolis, and Philadelphia. Small towns across rural America where the general store is still the heart of the community. Virtually every beloved neighborhood in every city in Europe, Asia, and Latin America. All of them mix small-scale retail with residential life, and all of them are considered more desirable, more livable, and more socially connected because of it.

Even here at home, the neighborhoods in our own metro area that command the highest prices and the longest waitlists for housing are the ones that already have walkable shops, cafés, and services mixed in among the homes. People are paying a premium to live the way most neighborhoods used to be organized by default.

## Sensible Rules, Not No Rules

Advocating for corner stores doesn't mean advocating for a free-for-all. Reasonable regulations should be part of the conversation. Limits on square footage. Requirements for architectural compatibility with surrounding homes. Restrictions on hours of operation. Clear standards for signage, lighting, and noise. Conditions that the store serve a primarily local, pedestrian-oriented customer base.

These safeguards can be written into the zoning code. They can be enforced. They can be adjusted over time based on real experience rather than hypothetical fears. What shouldn't happen is a blanket prohibition that treats a 1,000-square-foot neighborhood market the same as a Walmart.

## The Neighborhood We Could Be

Imagine walking three blocks on a Saturday morning to a small shop where the owner knows your name. You pick up some eggs, a newspaper, maybe a pint of locally made ice cream. You run into a neighbor you haven't seen in weeks. Your kids ride their bikes there to buy popsicles in the summer. The retired couple on the corner no longer has to ask for a ride to the store every time they run out of something.

That's not a fantasy of urban planning theory. That's what neighborhoods looked like for generations, and what many of the best neighborhoods in the world still look like today.

The corner store didn't destroy the American neighborhood. Its absence did. Let's fix the zoning code and bring it back.

# Keep the Lights On: Why Our Libraries Should Stay Open Later

**Our city's libraries close their doors at 6 p.m. on weekdays. That's a problem — and it's one we can fix.**

Walk past any branch of our public library system at 5:45 on a Tuesday evening, and you'll see the same dispiriting scene: staff politely ushering people toward the exits, students packing up half-finished homework, a parent juggling a toddler and an armful of books she barely had time to choose. The lights go dark, the doors lock, and a vital public resource goes to sleep just as much of the city is finally free to use it.

It is time for our library system to extend its evening hours to at least 9 p.m., and ideally later.

## Libraries That Close at Six Serve Only Some of Us

The current schedule operates as though the city is still living in 1955, when a single breadwinner left for a nine-to-five job and a stay-at-home spouse had the daylight hours free for errands. That world, if it ever truly existed for most families, is long gone. Today the majority of working adults don't clock out until 5 p.m. or later. Shift workers, gig workers, and those holding down multiple jobs often aren't available until well into the evening. By closing at six, we are telling these residents — disproportionately lower-income, disproportionately people of color — that the library isn't really for them.

A public institution funded by all taxpayers should be accessible to all taxpayers. Right now, it isn't.

## Students Need the Space

Talk to any middle- or high-school teacher in this city, and they will tell you the same thing: not every student has a quiet place to study at home. Overcrowded apartments, noisy siblings, unreliable internet, no desk — these aren't rare hardships. They are the everyday reality for thousands of young people who depend on the library as a second classroom. When the library closes at six, a student who has after-school activities, a part-time job, or family responsibilities loses access to the one free, safe, well-lit space designed for learning. The academic consequences are predictable and well-documented. Cities that have extended library hours — Chicago, Los Angeles, San Francisco — have seen measurable increases in youth program participation and homework completion rates at branches that stay open later.

## Safety Is a Benefit, Not a Cost

Critics of extended hours sometimes raise safety concerns. But consider the alternative. When libraries close early, the people who would have been inside reading, studying, and attending programs are instead out on the street or stuck in situations with fewer constructive options. Libraries are supervised, climate-controlled, staffed environments. They are among the safest public spaces in any city. Keeping them open longer doesn't create risk; it reduces it — particularly for vulnerable populations such as teenagers, the unhoused, and domestic-violence survivors seeking a refuge during evening hours.

## The Cost Is Modest; the Return Is Enormous

Yes, extended hours require funding — additional staff wages, utilities, and security. But the numbers are far less daunting than opponents suggest. A 2019 Urban Libraries Council analysis found that adding three evening hours to a mid-size branch costs roughly what a city spends repaving two blocks of roadway. We do not hesitate to keep parks lit, fire stations staffed, and community centers running at night. Libraries deserve the same commitment, because they deliver the same — arguably greater — public good.

Moreover, libraries with evening hours generate economic ripple effects. Small businesses near branches report increased foot traffic. Job seekers gain access to computers and résumé workshops after their current shifts end. ESL classes and citizenship-preparation programs can be scheduled at times that actually work for immigrant families. Every one of these outcomes strengthens the tax base and reduces demand on costlier social services down the line.

## A Library Card Shouldn't Come with a Curfew

At its core, this is a question about equity and values. We say we believe the public library is a cornerstone of democracy — a place where anyone, regardless of income, can access information, pursue education, and participate in community life. If we mean it, then we must ensure that "anyone" includes the nurse finishing a twelve-hour shift, the high-school junior working the register at a fast-food restaurant until seven, and the single father who can't get to the library before his children's bedtime under the current schedule.

A library that closes before most people eat dinner is a library that has made a choice about whom it serves. We can make a better choice.

Extend the hours. Keep the lights on. Let the library fulfill the promise we claim it represents.

# The Break Room Had a Clock, and Nobody Was Afraid of It

The first thing I noticed was the chair. Not because it was beautiful or special, but because it was there—a real chair, with a back and padding, behind the register where I'd be standing. "For when your feet get tired," said Denise, the shop steward, as casually as someone pointing out the bathroom. She must have seen my face, because she added, "You're allowed to sit. It's in the contract."

I was twenty-three. I'd been working since I was fifteen. I had never once been allowed to sit down.

---

Before that morning in September, I understood work the way a horse understands a harness. You put it on. It wore into you. Eventually it shaped you, and you stopped noticing where the straps cut. I'd done fast food, retail, warehouse picking, a summer of landscaping that left me browning and peeling in strips like old paint. I'd been scheduled for closes and then opens, called in on days off with the understanding that "no" was a word that got your hours cut to nothing. I'd been written up for leaning. I'd had a manager time my bathroom breaks with her phone.

I don't say this for pity. I say it because I need you to understand the world I thought was fixed, permanent, natural as gravity, so you can understand how strange it was to see a chair behind a register and feel the floor shift under me.

---

I got the job at the grocery store because my friend Marco worked there and told me they were hiring. "It's union," he said, like that was the selling point. I nodded, not really understanding what he meant. My parents hadn't been union. Nobody in my family talked about unions except my grandfather, and only when he was drinking, and only about how the Teamsters had gone soft. To me, "union" meant dues taken from your check—another hand in your pocket.

The store was a mid-size chain in the northeast, the kind with scuffed linoleum and hand-written signs over the deli counter. Nothing gleaming, nothing aspirational. I showed up at seven in the morning with my shirt tucked in too tight, the way you do when you want to signal that you're serious, that you won't cause problems, that you are grateful.

Denise met me by the time clock. She was maybe fifty, with short silver hair and reading glasses on a beaded chain. She had the energy of a woman who had seen a lot of new hires come through and was going to be patient about it. She shook my hand and said, "Welcome. Let me tell you how things work here."

What followed was the most disorienting forty-five minutes of my working life.

She walked me through the store, but not the way managers had walked me through jobs before—here's where we keep the mop, here's what happens if your drawer is short, here is the number to call if you can't make your shift and God help you if you use it. Denise told me those things too, but woven into every explanation was another layer, one I had no framework for: my rights.

I would get two fifteen-minute breaks and a thirty-minute lunch. These were non-negotiable. If a manager asked me to skip a break, I was to say no and then tell Denise. I would not be scheduled for a shift with less than ten hours between the end of one and the start of another. If I was asked to do work outside my job classification, I would be paid the higher rate. Overtime was time-and-a-half, always, no exceptions, no comp time, no "we'll make it up to you." There was a grievance procedure. There was a number I could call.

"You'll get a copy of the contract," she said. "Read it. It's not exciting, but it's yours."

---

That was the word that kept surfacing. *Yours.* The break was yours. The chair was yours. The contract was yours. I had never heard that language applied to a job. Work, in my experience, was something that belonged to the company, and you were grateful to be allowed near it.

I started on the register that morning, and for the first hour, I kept glancing at the chair. I didn't sit. Old habits. My body had been trained by years of vigilance—the posture of a person who assumes they are being watched and judged. I smiled at customers the way I'd always smiled. Too wide, too fast. The smile of someone who has been told that their attitude is the only thing between them and the parking lot.

Around ten o'clock, my feet started to ache—new shoes on hard floor, the usual. The woman on the register next to me, Pat, eased herself into her chair without ceremony, without guilt, without looking around first. She just sat. Like a person.

So I sat.

Nothing happened.

No one appeared from the back. No one made a note. The store continued. Cans beeped across scanners. Somewhere a kid was crying over a cereal box. Pat asked me how I was settling in. The clock on the wall ticked forward, and I understood, slowly, that the clock was not my enemy. It was just a clock.

---

I want to be careful here, because I'm not writing a fairy tale. The job was still a job. My back still hurt after long shifts. Some customers were awful. There was one manager—Dale—who visibly resented the contract and would test new hires with little provocations: asking you to stay "just a few minutes" past your shift, suggesting that break times were "flexible." Denise called this "the Dale routine" and told me to watch for it.

But the provocations themselves were proof of something. Dale couldn't just do what he wanted. There were limits on his power, and those limits weren't a suggestion or a favor—they were structural, contractual, enforceable. The first time I saw Denise walk into the back office on behalf of a coworker who'd been shortchanged on overtime, I felt something I can only describe as awe. She wasn't asking. She was holding a document and pointing to a clause, and the manager across the desk from her was listening because he had to.

I had never seen a worker make management *listen* before. I had only ever seen the reverse.

---

There was a moment, my third or fourth week, when the truth of the thing really landed. I was in the break room eating a sandwich, and a few of the longer-tenured employees were talking about the upcoming contract negotiation. They were debating whether to push for a higher wage floor or better vision coverage. They disagreed. They argued. One guy, Terrence, was passionate about the vision plan because his daughter needed glasses and the current coverage was lousy. A woman named Araceli made the case for wages, because a higher floor would help the newest workers the most, and the newest workers, she said, were always the most vulnerable.

They were talking about their working conditions as something they had a hand in shaping.

This was the revolution. Not a dramatic one. Not the kind that makes the news. Just people in a beige room with a coffee maker and a humming refrigerator, talking about what their labor was worth and expecting to have a say in the answer. I sat there with my sandwich and thought about every job I'd ever had—every time I'd swallowed a complaint, worked through a fever, laughed at a manager's bad joke because I was afraid. I thought about the fact that I had believed, truly believed, that this was simply what work was. That the discomfort was the point, and the endurance of it was the price of survival.

I had mistaken powerlessness for maturity.

---

I stayed at that grocery store for almost four years. I went to union meetings. I voted on contracts. I once filed a grievance when Dale tried to change my schedule without the required notice, and I won, and the world didn't end. Dale was annoyed. I survived his annoyance. It turns out that surviving your boss's annoyance is a skill, and like most skills, you get better at it when you have backup.

I left eventually, as people do. I went back to school, moved, built a different life. But I never lost the understanding that settled into me on that first day, the one that started with a chair:

Work does not have to hurt you to count. You are not lazy for wanting to sit down. Your time has a value that you did not set and should not have to beg for. The negotiation between the people who do the work and the people who profit from it is not a settled matter—it is an ongoing one, and you are allowed, entitled, *expected* to show up for it.

Before that job, I thought dignity at work was something you earned through silence and endurance. After it, I understood that dignity at work is something you build—together, out loud, with a contract in one hand and a chair to sit in when your feet get tired.

# The 11:47

For a year, I rode the same bus home. The 11:47, Route 19, northbound from downtown. I could tell you things about that bus that I have no business knowing—that the third window on the driver's side didn't close all the way, that the overhead light above the back row flickered at every stop, that the seats smelled faintly of something between wet wool and cough syrup, a smell I eventually stopped noticing and then, months later, started almost missing when it wasn't there.

I started the job in January. Closing shift at a restaurant where I expedited orders, which is a generous way of saying I stood under heat lamps and yelled. By the time I wiped down the pass and clocked out, the streets had that particular late-night emptiness where every sound carries too far. My footsteps on the sidewalk. The buzz of the crosswalk signal changing for no one. The bus shelter with its cracked plastic walls, half of them tagged with a Sharpie someone never seemed to run out of.

The first few weeks, I hated it. The waiting. The cold that came up through the soles of my shoes and settled in my knees. The way the bus sometimes ran four minutes late, sometimes nine, once seventeen, and you could never know which it would be, so you just stood there performing a private calculation about whether you'd been forgotten. I'd check my phone, refresh the transit app, watch the little blue icon of my bus creep along streets I couldn't see, and feel a small, irrational fury at how slowly it moved—as if the driver were personally withholding something from me.

But you can't sustain that kind of resentment. Not over months. The body adapts. The mind adapts. And somewhere around March, something shifted. I stopped checking the app. I just stood there and waited, and the waiting became a kind of pause I hadn't known I needed—a seam in the day where nothing was required of me, where I wasn't expediting anything, where I was just a person standing in the cold, breathing.

---

The regulars revealed themselves slowly.

There was the woman I thought of as the Reader. She boarded two stops after me, always carrying a tote bag with a library book in it. She read the whole ride with a penlight she clipped to the pages, the little beam trembling with every pothole. I never saw the covers. I wanted to ask her what she was reading but sensed that the question would break something—some contract of mutual silence we'd entered into without negotiation.

There was the man in the high-visibility vest who smelled like fryer grease, same as me. We recognized each other the way animals of the same species do. A nod. Nothing more. He got off four stops before mine, always pulling the cord at exactly the right moment, never needing to look up to know where he was. I envied that. It took me weeks to develop the same internal geography—the feeling in your body that says *this is your corner* before your eyes confirm it.

There was the kid—eighteen, maybe twenty—who got on around April and rode all the way to the end of the line. He wore massive headphones and bobbed his head to whatever was playing, occasionally mouthing words with the unselfconscious passion of someone who believed he was alone. Sometimes I'd catch the faintest leak of bass from across the aisle, a tinny pulse that made me imagine whole songs I'd never hear. He disappeared in July. I don't know why. People disappear from buses and you never learn the reason. They get a car. They move. They switch shifts. They simply stop, and the space where they sat becomes just a seat again.

---

There's a particular quality to a city seen from a late-night bus. The storefronts are dark, but some of them leave a single light on deep inside—a back office, a storeroom—and for a moment, as the bus passes, you see into a version of the place its daytime customers never know. The laundromat with its row of machines standing open like mouths. The nail salon with its chairs pushed against the wall, cotton balls still scattered on the counter. The taqueria where someone is mopping, always mopping, as if the floor will never be clean enough.

I began to think of these glimpses as a kind of privilege. Not because they were beautiful, exactly, but because they were honest. The city at midnight doesn't perform. It sags. It leaves its makeup on the counter. The late-night bus moves through a world that has stopped trying to sell you anything, and there is a deep relief in that.

The bus itself felt like a room outside of rooms—not home, not work, not public in the way a sidewalk is public. It had walls and a ceiling and warmth, and inside it, strangers sat together in a silence that was neither hostile nor friendly but something rarer: neutral. Shared. We were all just going home. That was the whole story. Every one of us had somewhere else to be, and the bus was the in-between, and in the in-between, nothing was expected.

I've read essays about liminal spaces. I've seen the term used to describe airports, hotel hallways, parking garages at dusk. But none of those spaces moved. The bus was liminal *and* in motion, carrying you from one life to another while you sat still, and the stillness inside the motion was the thing I came to need—the fifteen minutes where I was, technically, nowhere.

---

In June, the regular driver changed.

The first driver had been a heavyset man who played talk radio at a volume just loud enough to hear but not loud enough to follow. He never greeted anyone. He never said goodnight. He drove with the bored competence of someone who could do it asleep, and maybe sometimes did. I liked him in the way you like a ceiling fan—you don't think about it until it's gone.

The new driver was a woman with short gray hair who said "watch your step" to every person who boarded, even the ones who had clearly been boarding buses for decades. At first, I found it grating. Then I realized she meant it. She was genuinely concerned about the step. It was tall and the rubber tread was worn smooth and one night in August I watched a man with a cane nearly fall, and she was out of her seat before anyone else moved, her hand on his arm, steady.

After that, I heard her differently. *Watch your step.* It wasn't a script. It was a warning delivered with the same sincerity every time, which meant she saw every person who climbed aboard, really saw them, which is more than most of us manage at noon, let alone at midnight.

I started saying thank you when I got off. I'd never done that with the first driver. I don't know what that says about me.

---

Somewhere in October—deep enough into the year that the routine had become invisible, the way your own heartbeat is invisible—I had what I can only describe as a moment.

It was raining. Not hard, just steady, the kind of rain that turns every window into a sheet of moving light. The bus was warm. The Reader was reading. The man in the vest was in his seat. A new regular, a young woman with scrubs and white clogs, was asleep with her head against the window, her breath fogging a small circle on the glass. The driver said *watch your step* to no one—we were between stops—or maybe to all of us, a general benediction.

And I felt, suddenly and without explanation, that I was exactly where I was supposed to be. Not in some grand, destined sense. In a simple one. I was a body on a bus, moving through the rain, surrounded by other bodies doing the same thing I was doing, and the sheer ordinary fact of it felt, for a moment, like enough. More than enough. Like the most coherent thing in my life.

I know how that sounds. I know that a bus is just a bus. That the 11:47 was not a cathedral or a therapist's office or a poem. It was a municipal vehicle with bad shocks and an ad for a personal injury lawyer above the back door. But I think that's exactly why the moment landed the way it did. It wasn't elevated. It wasn't special. It was just true—a real place full of real people at a real hour, none of us performing, all of us tired, all of us almost home.

---

I left the restaurant job in January, almost exactly a year after I started. A better opportunity. Better hours. I'd be done by six, home by seven, eating dinner at a table instead of standing over the kitchen trash can at half past midnight.

My last ride on the 11:47 was unremarkable. The Reader was there. The driver said *watch your step.* I sat in my usual seat—fourth row, left side, aisle—and watched the storefronts pass. When my stop came, I pulled the cord with the timing I'd earned over twelve months, and the bus eased over, and I stepped off into the cold.

I said thank you. The doors closed. The bus pulled away, its taillights blurring in the dark, and I stood on the sidewalk for a moment longer than I needed to, watching it go.

I take a different bus now, an earlier one, in daylight, crowded with people on their phones. It's fine. It gets me where I'm going. But sometimes at night, if I'm still up at 11:47, I feel a small pull—not nostalgia exactly, but something adjacent. A awareness that out there, right now, the bus is running. The Reader is reading. The driver is saying *watch your step.* The city is sliding past the windows, dark and unperforming and honest, and a version of me I no longer am is sitting in the fourth row, left side, almost home, suspended in the beautiful ordinary nowhere of the in-between.

# The Deep End

The pool at Willowbrook Park was built in 1966 with federal recreation funds and local bond money, and by the time I knew it, the concrete deck had cracked in a way that mapped the decades. Long seams ran from the chain-link fence to the gutter edge, filled with black caulk that softened in July heat until it stuck to the balls of your feet. You could read the history of a town's priorities in those cracks — which ones had been patched and which ones hadn't, which years someone in a city office had cared enough to submit a maintenance request and which years the request sat in a drawer.

I spent every summer of my childhood at that pool, from the time I was five until I was sixteen, and what I remember most is not the swimming. What I remember is the waiting.

---

The pool opened at noon. This was a fact as fixed and holy as anything in scripture. By eleven-thirty, a line formed along the fence on Garfield Avenue — kids on bikes, mothers with wagons full of towels and sandwiches in plastic bags, a few old men in lawn chairs who had no intention of getting in the water but who came to sit in the shade of the cinderblock bathhouse and listen to a transistor radio. The line was the first thing the pool revealed about us. It showed who had nowhere else to go.

There was no country club in our town, no backyard pools to speak of. A few families on the north side had aboveground pools — those flimsy aluminum-walled circles that turned green by August — but these were understood to be private kingdoms, invitation only, and the invitations were capricious. The public pool was the alternative, the democratic option, the place where a dollar fifty and a wristband meant you were equal to everyone, at least until five o'clock.

But of course you were not equal to everyone. The pool taught me this, too.

---

There was a hierarchy, and it arranged itself as naturally as water finding its level. The deep end belonged to the teenagers. This was not written anywhere, but everyone knew it. The teenagers colonized the two diving boards — one low, one high — and the area of blue water beneath them, and they performed. The boys did cannonballs and backflips. The girls sat on the pool's edge with their legs in the water and watched, or pretended not to watch, which was its own kind of performance. The게 deep end was a theater, and the admission price was the nerve to climb the high dive's ladder while everyone looked up at you.

The shallow end was for the small children and the uncertain. Mothers stood in water up to their thighs, holding infants against their hips. Kids in inflatable arm floats bobbed like little buoys. The middle of the pool — that democratic stretch of four-to-five-foot water — was where everyone else churned together: the lap swimmers who had given up on laps because there were no lanes, the clusters of middle-school kids playing Marco Polo, the dads who floated on their backs with their eyes closed and their bellies breaking the surface like soft islands.

And along the fence, in the grass, the people who didn't swim. They were mostly older, mostly women, and they sat in folding chairs with quilted seat cushions and thermoses of sweet tea and they talked. They talked for hours. They talked with a seriousness and an intimacy that I now understand was the whole point of their coming. The pool was their living room. The noise of children screaming was their background music. They solved each other's problems, or they didn't, but they witnessed them, and sometimes witnessing is the thing.

---

The town was called Drescher, Pennsylvania, population eight thousand, give or take, depending on whether you counted the trailer park on Route 30 or the new development past the Wawa. It had been a mill town, then a nothing town, then a town trying to become something again. There was a Main Street with a barber shop, a pizza place, a Dollar General that used to be a hardware store, and a funeral home that always looked busy. The high school mascot was a ram. The biggest employer was a distribution warehouse for a company that sold industrial cleaning supplies. If you wanted to understand Drescher — not the way a census understood it, but the way you understand a person by sitting with them long enough — you went to the pool.

At the pool, you could see that the town was poorer than it wanted to admit. You could see it in the swimsuits. Kids wore cutoff shorts and oversized t-shirts instead of trunks. Women wore suits that were four, five, six summers old, the elastic blown, the colors faded to a memory of what they'd been. Nobody said anything about this. Bodies were bodies, suits were suits, and vanity was a luxury that cost more than a dollar fifty.

You could see that the town was whiter than it thought it was, but not as white as it used to be. The Gonzalez family came every day, all seven of them, and the mother packed enough food for a siege. The Ahmeds came on weekends. A Black family named Carter had three daughters who could outswim every boy in the deep end, and the youngest, Trina, did a backflip off the high dive at age nine that made a lifeguard stand up from his chair and clap before remembering he was supposed to blow his whistle. These families were part of the pool. But they were also, in ways I only recognized later, navigating it — reading the temperature of the deck in ways I never had to.

I remember one afternoon when Mrs. Gonzalez spread a blanket in the grass near the deep end, in the spot where a group of older white women had established a kind of base camp every day for years. Nothing happened. That is exactly what I mean. Nothing happened, and Mrs. Gonzalez moved to a spot closer to the fence, and the older women did not say a single word, and nothing happened. It was the kind of nothing that is actually something, and the pool was full of these moments if you watched for them.

---

The lifeguards were gods. There is no other way to describe the station they occupied in the cosmology of a public pool. They sat in elevated chairs with zinc oxide on their noses, and they held dominion over all of it — the running (no running), the roughhousing (no roughhousing), the length of time you could hang on the lane rope before they blew the whistle and pointed at you. They were sixteen, seventeen, eighteen years old. They had the deep tans and the boredom of minor royalty.

The head lifeguard the summer I was twelve was a guy named Sean Kessler. He was eighteen, just graduated from Drescher High, and he had a body like the guys on the covers of my mother's fitness magazines. He never yelled. He just pointed, or shook his head once, and whatever you were doing, you stopped doing it. I wanted not to be him but to be known by him, to have him nod at me as I climbed the ladder to the high dive, to be a person he recognized.

This was another thing the pool taught me — that longing has a structure. That desire organizes itself around the nearest available object of admiration, and at a public pool in a small town, the nearest available objects were lifeguards and teenagers who could do backflips.

Sean Kessler went to community college in the fall and then, according to the barbershop gossip network, dropped out and got a job at the warehouse. I saw him the next summer at the pool. He wasn't lifeguarding. He was just there, in the water, like the rest of us. He seemed smaller.

---

Every hour, on the hour, the lifeguards blew the long whistle — two blasts, which meant adult swim. All children had to leave the water. This was ostensibly so the adults could have fifteen minutes of peace, but very few adults actually swam during adult swim. The real purpose, we all understood, was to make the children rest. To pull them from the water before their lips turned blue. To give their mothers a chance to force a sandwich into them.

During adult swim, we lay on towels and shivered and stared at the water like exiles staring at a homeland. Fifteen minutes felt like an hour. But I see now that adult swim was one of the few moments when the pool revealed its best self — its capacity for collective care. Someone, at some point, had decided that children would not be allowed to exhaust themselves to the point of danger, and this decision was enforced every hour, without exception, by nineteen-year-olds earning seven dollars an hour. It was a public health measure disguised as a nuisance, and it worked.

---

The summer I was fourteen, the town voted down a bond measure that would have funded repairs to the pool. I didn't understand what this meant at the time. I understood only that the drinking fountain near the bathhouse stopped working and was never fixed, that one of the diving boards was removed and the bolts were left in the concrete like pulled teeth, and that the hours were cut. The pool opened later and closed earlier. It felt like a punishment, though I didn't know who was being punished, or for what.

My father explained it in the way he explained most things — sitting in his recliner, not looking at me, talking to the television. "People don't want to pay for things other people use," he said.

This was not entirely true. People paid for the roads, for the fire department, for the high school football team's new uniforms. But the pool was different. The pool was used by the people who couldn't afford not to use it, and this made it easy to underfund. This was the deepest thing the pool revealed about the town: that generosity was conditional, that the public was only as public as the voters decided it should be, and that when money was scarce, the first things to crack were the things that served the people with the least.

---

I left Drescher at eighteen, the way most people left Drescher — with a duffel bag and a vague plan and the feeling that I was escaping something, though I couldn't have named what. I went to college two hours away, then to a city, then to another city, the way you do. I came back for holidays, then for funerals, then hardly at all.

A few years ago, passing through on my way somewhere else, I stopped at Willowbrook Park. The pool was closed. Not for the season — closed. Permanently. A sign on the fence said the facility had been decommissioned due to structural concerns. The concrete deck had buckled in places. Weeds grew through the cracks. The pool itself had been drained, and the basin was filled with a layer of dead leaves and standing rainwater the color of coffee.

I stood at the fence and looked at it for a long time, longer than made sense. A woman walking a dog stopped and stood with me, and we both looked at the empty pool, and she said, "My kids grew up in there."

"Me too," I said.

She told me that there had been talk of filling it in and building a pickleball court. She said this the way you'd report a terminal diagnosis — with resignation and the faintest edge of black humor. We both knew a pickleball court was not a pool. A pickleball court did not hold the bodies of an entire town. A pickleball court did not teach you to hold your breath, or jump from a height that scared you, or sit next to someone who was different from you in four feet of water on a day so hot that none of the differences mattered as much as the water.

---

I don't want to make the pool into something it wasn't. It was a rectangle of chlorinated water in a small town. The bathrooms smelled terrible. Someone was always bleeding from a scraped knee. The water was too warm by late July and tasted of chemicals and sunscreen. The PA system played the same classic rock station all summer, and to this day I cannot hear "Born to Run" without smelling chlorine.

But a public pool is a commitment a town makes to itself. It says: we believe that cool water on a hot day is not a privilege. It says: we will maintain a place where people can be together, without buying anything, without producing anything, just being bodies in water. It says: your children are worth the upkeep.

When a town lets its pool crack and drain and fill with leaves, it is saying something else. It may not mean to, but it is.

---

There is a moment I return to whenever I think about the pool. I was maybe ten. It was the middle of the afternoon on the hottest day of the summer. The pool was so full that you could barely move in the water; bodies everywhere, elbows and knees, the sound enormous — splashing, screaming, music, whistles. I was treading water in the middle of the pool, in that democratic no-man's-land between the shallow and the deep, and for a few seconds I stopped moving and just let myself be held up by the noise and the crowd and the water. I couldn't see the bottom. I didn't need to. The water held me. The town, in its imperfect and underfunded and cracking way, held me.

I didn't know then that this was something worth noticing, much less preserving. I thought it was just summer. I thought it would always be there.

# The Crossing

## A Profile of Captain Dale Erickson

The first trip of the day is still dark. At 5:15 on a November morning, the engine of the M/V *Steadfast* turns over with a shudder that Dale Erickson says he can feel in his molars. He has been making this sound happen for thirty-one years. He stands in the wheelhouse with a thermos of coffee he filled at home and a weather report he already knows is wrong, and he guides forty-two hundred tons of steel away from the dock at Corden, Washington, toward the town of Marisport, six nautical miles across Shale Bay.

The crossing takes twenty-eight minutes. On a clear day, he can see the Marisport terminal before he has fully left Corden's breakwater. The two towns sit facing each other like old neighbors on opposite sides of a road, close enough to seem connected but separated by water deep and cold enough to demand respect. By car, the drive between them takes an hour and forty minutes along the winding coastal highway that climbs up over Decker Ridge and back down. By ferry, it takes less time than most people spend on lunch.

Erickson has made this crossing roughly 43,000 times. He arrived at that number himself, on a slow Tuesday afternoon several years ago, multiplying and scribbling on the back of a maintenance log. He seemed neither proud nor disturbed by the figure. "I just wanted to see what it looked like written down," he said.

---

He is sixty-three years old, broad through the shoulders, with a gray mustache he has worn since his twenties and hands that look like they were designed for the specific purpose of gripping a wheel in bad weather. He speaks slowly and with a kind of deliberateness that can be mistaken for reluctance. He is not reluctant. He simply doesn't say things he hasn't thought about first, a quality that makes him an outlier in most conversations and, by his own account, a difficult man to be married to.

His wife, Jeanne, might dispute that characterization, though perhaps not forcefully. They have been married for thirty-seven years. She works as a bookkeeper for a marine supply company in Corden and has ridden the ferry exactly four times, all for dental appointments in Marisport when her regular dentist in Corden retired. "I see enough of that boat from the kitchen window," she said.

The Ericksons live in a cedar-shingled house on the hill above the Corden terminal. From their living room, Jeanne can watch the *Steadfast* come and go all day. She said she used to watch it more in the early years. Now the ferry's movement has become something like a clock on the wall — present, functional, noticed mainly when it stops.

---

Erickson grew up in Corden, the second of four children born to a fisherman and a school secretary. The town had a population of around 2,200 then; it has a population of around 2,100 now. He played basketball in high school without distinction, worked summers at a boatyard, and, after a single semester at a community college in Bellingham that he describes as "a misunderstanding," came home and got a job as a deckhand on the ferry system.

He was twenty years old. The route between Corden and Marisport was, at the time, operated by an older vessel called the *Klickitat Bay*, captained by a man named Norm Ostrander, who had been making the crossing since 1965 and who wore the same brown jacket every day regardless of the season. Erickson describes Ostrander as the most competent and least talkative person he has ever known.

"Norm could dock that boat in a crosswind like he was parking a car," Erickson said. "And he could go four hours without saying a word that wasn't about the boat."

Erickson worked as a deckhand for three years, earned his mate's license, then spent another five years as a relief operator on various routes around the sound. In 1993, when the state replaced the *Klickitat Bay* with the newly built *Steadfast* and Ostrander retired, Erickson applied for and received the captain's position on the Corden-Marisport run. He was thirty-two.

He has not applied for another position since.

---

This is the part of Dale Erickson's story that tends to puzzle people, particularly people who do not live in places like Corden or Marisport. The state ferry system operates dozens of routes, some longer, some more scenic, some carrying more passengers and offering more prestige. Captains with his seniority can bid on nearly any run they want. Over the years, he has been asked — by supervisors, by colleagues, once by a reporter from a Seattle magazine — why he has never pursued a different route, a promotion, a change.

His answers vary slightly depending on his mood but tend to arrive at the same place. "I know this water," he said on a recent morning, standing in the wheelhouse as the *Steadfast* pulled away from Corden into a flat gray calm. "I know what the current does off Halleck Point in an ebb tide. I know where the crab pots sit in September. I know that when the wind comes from the south-southeast at more than fifteen knots, there's a chop about two-thirds of the way across that can come up fast enough to knock a coffee cup off a table. I don't know why I'd want to go learn somebody else's water."

There is, perhaps, a simpler answer beneath that practical one. Erickson is a man who lives in the town where he was born and does the work he started doing when he was barely old enough to do it. He has witnessed the same stretch of water in every available condition — glassy, violent, fogged in, frozen at the edges, streaked with bioluminescence on rare summer nights — and he does not appear to have grown tired of looking at it. Whether this represents some deep contentment or simply a lack of imagination is a question that probably depends on who is asking.

---

The two towns his ferry connects have a complicated interdependence. Corden has the larger grocery store and the only hardware store. Marisport has the hospital, the community college satellite campus, and a cluster of restaurants that cater to summer tourists. Residents of each town rely on the ferry for things the other town provides. When the *Steadfast* goes down for maintenance — typically two weeks each winter — the disruption is immediate and felt sharply on both sides of the bay.

Erickson occupies an unusual position in this ecosystem. He is, in a functional sense, the bridge between the two communities, yet he belongs entirely to one of them. He knows the Marisport terminal the way a bus driver knows a stop — thoroughly, from a professional distance. He rarely gets off the boat there.

His passengers, however, he knows with an intimacy that would be impossible on a larger route. The Corden-Marisport run carries an average of about 180 vehicles per day. On a Wednesday in February, it might carry sixty. These are not strangers. They are commuters and contractors and teenagers driving to class and elderly couples going to medical appointments. Erickson knows their trucks, their schedules, their habits.

"There's a guy, runs a plumbing business in Marisport, takes the 7:10 every morning," Erickson said. "He's been doing that for — I don't know, fifteen years. If I don't see his van in the lane by 7:05, I know something's off. Last spring he didn't show up for three days. Turned out he'd thrown his back out." He paused. "I don't know his last name."

This is the texture of his knowledge: wide but selectively shallow, built on observation rather than conversation. He recognizes dogs in car windows. He can tell from the weight of the vehicle load whether school is in session. He once noticed that a regular passenger had stopped driving her own car and started riding in her daughter's, and correctly intuited, without being told, that the woman's eyesight had gotten too poor to drive.

---

The physical toll of the work is not dramatic but cumulative. Erickson has some hearing loss in his left ear, which he attributes to three decades of engine noise and wind. His knees ache in cold weather. He takes blood pressure medication, as do, he noted dryly, most of the men he graduated high school with. The repetitive motion of docking — the particular sequence of throttle, wheel, and thruster adjustments that brings a 200-foot vessel to rest against a wooden pier — has given him a chronic stiffness in his right shoulder that he manages with ibuprofen and what he calls "ignoring it."

The mental toll is harder to measure. He acknowledges that the repetition can produce a kind of numbness, a state where the crossings blur together until a day feels like a single long transit rather than twelve distinct ones. He fights this by paying close attention to the water, which, he insists, is never the same twice. "People think this is boring work," he said. "People don't look at the water."

He has had three serious incidents in thirty-one years. In 1998, a sailboat under auxiliary power crossed his bow in dense fog, close enough that the skipper later said he could read the *Steadfast*'s draft markings. In 2007, a mechanical failure left the ferry without steering for approximately ninety seconds in the middle of the channel. And in 2014, during a winter storm that probably should have canceled the run, a wave broke over the car deck and shattered the windshield of a pickup truck.

No one was injured in any of these events. Erickson does not discuss them casually. When pressed, he said only that the 2007 steering failure was the longest ninety seconds of his life and that he went home that night and sat on his porch for an hour without going inside.

---

The question of retirement surfaces, as it does for anyone his age in a physically demanding job. His employer requires a medical certification every two years after sixty, and he has passed each one without issue. He is eligible for full pension benefits. Jeanne, he says, has opinions on the matter that she expresses with increasing frequency.

On the morning I rode with him, he was asked directly when he planned to stop. He looked out at the bay, which was the color of an old nickel, and took long enough to answer that I thought he might not.

"I think about it," he said finally. "I think about it the way you think about weather that's still a few days out. You can see it coming. You know it's real. But it hasn't arrived yet, so there's no sense acting on it."

The *Steadfast* was approaching Marisport. He set down his coffee and placed both hands on the wheel, beginning the sequence of small adjustments he has performed tens of thousands of times. The pier grew larger in the window. A deckhand's voice came over the radio. Erickson gave a one-word response.

The ferry settled against the dock with a gentleness that belied its size, and the ramp came down, and the cars began to roll off into a town that was not his, and within minutes they would load again, and he would turn the *Steadfast* around, and cross back.

# Carrying the Weight

## A Profile of Public Defender Maria Sandoval

---

The fluorescent lights in Courtroom 4B of the Mecklenburg County Courthouse have a faint, insistent flicker that most people stop noticing after a few minutes. Maria Sandoval noticed it nine years ago, on her first day as an assistant public defender, and she notices it still. It is the kind of small, persistent irritation that reminds her she is paying attention — that she has not, despite every institutional pressure to do so, gone numb.

It is 8:47 on a Tuesday morning in late October, and Sandoval is standing near the defense table with a legal pad full of handwriting so compressed it looks, from a distance, like a single dark smear. She is wearing a navy blazer she bought at a consignment shop on South Boulevard and flats she can stand in for ten hours. In thirteen minutes, the docket will begin. She is responsible for eleven of the twenty-six cases on it.

Eleven cases. Eleven human beings whose names she memorized over the weekend from files she carried home in a rolling crate, reading them at her kitchen table while her ten-year-old daughter, Lucia, did math homework across from her. Eleven people who, if asked, might not be able to pick her out of a lineup, because she met most of them for the first time in a holding area forty-five minutes ago.

"You learn to do triage," Sandoval says. "You learn which conversation needs fifteen minutes and which one you can do in three. You learn to read a person's face for what they're not telling you. And then you go in there and you fight like they're your only client, because in that moment, they are."

---

**Maria Elena Sandoval** is forty-one years old. She is a senior assistant public defender in the criminal division of a mid-size Southern city where the population has boomed, the court system has not kept pace, and the annual per-attorney caseload in the public defender's office routinely exceeds national guidelines by a factor of two or more. The American Bar Association recommends that a criminal defense attorney handle no more than 150 felonies per year, or 400 misdemeanors. In a typical year, Sandoval carries between 250 and 300 active cases at any given time — a rolling, churning mass of felonies and high-level misdemeanors that she manages with a combination of obsessive organization, dark humor, and what her colleagues describe as a nearly inhuman stamina.

She does not think of herself as heroic. She is, in fact, mildly annoyed by the word.

"People hear 'public defender' and they either think you're a saint or you're incompetent," she says, pulling into the courthouse parking garage in a twelve-year-old Honda Civic with a child's booster seat in the back and case files in the passenger seat. "I'm neither. I'm a lawyer. I went to law school. I passed the bar. I do the same job a private attorney does, except I do it for people who can't pay, and I do it with fewer resources, and I do it more often."

She pauses.

"A *lot* more often."

---

The path to the public defender's office was, for Sandoval, neither accidental nor romantic. She grew up in a working-class neighborhood on the west side of the city, the daughter of a commercial painter and a home health aide, both immigrants from El Salvador. Her father was arrested once, when she was fourteen, for driving without a license — a common predicament for undocumented workers in a state that did not then offer any path to legal driving status. She remembers sitting in the courtroom gallery. She remembers the court-appointed attorney who represented him spending what felt like less than two minutes on his case. She remembers her father being fined $200, which was roughly what the family spent on groceries in a month.

"That lawyer might have been perfectly fine," Sandoval says now, carefully. "He might have gotten the best possible outcome. But I remember the *speed* of it. My father didn't speak much English. He didn't understand what was happening. And no one explained it to him. No one even tried."

She went to UNC Charlotte on a full scholarship, then to the University of North Carolina School of Law, where she graduated in the top quarter of her class — a credential that, she is quick to point out, could have opened doors at any number of private firms. She chose instead to interview at the public defender's office during a hiring cycle so tight that the starting salary was $42,000 a year.

Today she makes $71,500. A first-year associate at a mid-tier private firm in the same city makes roughly $95,000.

"I'm not going to pretend the money doesn't matter," she says. "I have a kid. I have student loans. But I also know what it feels like to sit in a courtroom and feel invisible, and I decided a long time ago that my job was going to be making sure that doesn't happen to other people."

---

A Tuesday in Sandoval's life has a rhythm that sounds, when she describes it, like the schedule of an emergency-room physician — which is, in some ways, exactly what she is.

She arrives at the office by 7:15, sometimes earlier. The office itself is a suite of rooms on the third floor of a county building four blocks from the courthouse. The carpet is the color of old putty. The desks are pressed-wood composites from a government surplus catalog. There is one investigator shared among fourteen attorneys. There is no in-house social worker, despite the fact that a significant portion of the office's clients suffer from mental illness, addiction, or both.

By 7:30, she is reviewing the day's files — reading police reports, scanning discovery, checking for motions deadlines she may have missed. She makes notes in a shorthand she developed herself, a system of symbols and abbreviations that allows her to distill a case to its essential questions in a few square inches of paper: *What does the state have? What don't they have? What does my client want? What can I realistically get?*

By 8:00, she is walking to the courthouse. By 8:15, she is in the holding area behind the courtrooms — a cinder-block corridor with benches bolted to the floor and a smell she describes as "industrial cleaner over despair" — meeting clients. Some she knows well. Many she is meeting for the first time at a bond hearing or arraignment. She introduces herself. She explains what is about to happen. She asks if they have questions. She listens.

"The listening is the part that gets cut first when you're overwhelmed," she says. "And it's the part that matters most. Half the time, what a client needs isn't a legal strategy. It's someone who looks them in the eye and says, 'I hear you. I'm going to do everything I can.'"

By 9:00, court is in session. The morning docket moves fast — a procession of names and charges and continuances and plea offers that an outside observer might find difficult to distinguish from one another, but which represent, to Sandoval, twenty-six discrete human crises. She moves between the defense table, the hallway where she negotiates with prosecutors, and the gallery where clients' families wait with expressions that range from terrified to furious to simply blank.

On this particular Tuesday, she argues a bond reduction for a twenty-two-year-old man charged with breaking and entering. He has no prior felony convictions. He has a job — or had one, until he was arrested. He has a three-year-old son. The state is asking for $25,000 secured bond, which means he would need to come up with $2,500 for a bondsman — money his family does not have.

Sandoval stands and speaks for four minutes. She does not raise her voice. She cites her client's ties to the community, his employment history, his lack of flight risk. She notes that the evidence in the case is largely circumstantial. She asks for a $5,000 unsecured bond — meaning her client would be released on the promise to pay if he fails to appear, rather than being required to pay cash upfront.

The judge sets bond at $10,000 secured. A compromise. The client's mother, sitting in the second row, begins to cry silently.

In the hallway afterward, Sandoval stands with the mother for a few minutes. She explains the next steps. She gives her a phone number for a bail fund. She says she will check on the case Thursday. The mother grasps both of Sandoval's hands and says something in Spanish that Sandoval does not repeat to me later, except to say: "She thanked me. But I didn't get her son home."

---

The crisis in public defense in America is not new, but it has, in recent years, begun to receive the kind of attention that suggests the country is finally reckoning with a system it has been content to underfund for decades. In 2023, the American Bar Association published a comprehensive study finding that public defender offices nationwide are staffed at roughly half the level needed to provide constitutionally adequate representation. In several states, public defenders have filed lawsuits against their own governments, arguing that their caseloads are so extreme that they cannot ethically fulfill their obligations under the Sixth Amendment.

Sandoval's office has not filed such a suit, though she says the conversation has come up. The problem, she explains, is practical: filing a lawsuit takes time and energy that the attorneys in her office simply do not have.

"We're drowning," she says, and then catches herself, because she does not like the metaphor. "No. Drowning is passive. We're not drowning. We're swimming. We're swimming as hard as we can. But the water keeps rising, and nobody's building a bigger boat."

The consequences of the overload are tangible and specific. Sandoval estimates that on her current caseload, she spends an average of six to eight hours per case on felonies that, by any reasonable standard, deserve thirty or forty. She has gone to trial on cases where she would have liked another week — or another month — to investigate. She has advised clients to accept plea deals not because the deals were good, but because the alternative was waiting in jail for six months for a trial date.

"I tell them the truth," she says. "I say, 'Here's the offer. Here's what the evidence looks like. Here's what could happen at trial. And here's how long you'd be sitting in jail waiting for that trial, because the docket is backed up, and I can't control that.' And then they decide. But I'd be lying if I said the system doesn't push people toward pleas."

She is quiet for a moment.

"That's not justice. That's logistics."

---

There is a case Sandoval talks about when she wants to explain why she stays.

Three years ago, she represented a woman named Denise — she declines to give the last name — who was charged with felony possession of a controlled substance. Denise was forty-four. She had a long history of addiction and a criminal record that stretched back two decades, most of it drug-related. She was facing a potential sentence of two to three years in state prison. The assistant district attorney offered a plea: twelve to eighteen months, recommended.

Sandoval dug into the file. She requested the body-camera footage from the arresting officer and watched it three times. She noticed something: the officer had not articulated a clear reason for the initial stop. The traffic violation cited in the report — an expired registration tag — was contradicted by the footage, which appeared to show a valid tag. Sandoval filed a motion to suppress the evidence obtained during the stop. It took her two weeks to write it, most of the work done between 9:00 p.m. and midnight.

The judge granted the motion. The charges were dismissed.
