The team acted quickly once the problem was identified, and we are grateful for the patience employees and customers showed throughout the disruption. That patience is not something we take for granted, and it is not a resource we intend to draw on again due to a preventable failure.

If you have questions about today's incident or concerns about your work being affected, please reach out to your manager or directly to IT Operations.

---

**IT Operations & Engineering Leadership**
[Company Name]
[Contact Information]

---
*This memo is intended for internal distribution only. Please do not share externally.*

# INTERNAL MEMORANDUM

**TO:** All Employees
**FROM:** Sarah Chen, Chief People Officer
**DATE:** October 14, 2024
**RE:** New Remote Work Policy — Effective January 1, 2025

---

## Overview

After several months of listening sessions, manager feedback, and a company-wide survey in which 94 of you participated, we are formalizing a new remote work policy that takes effect on **January 1, 2025**. This memo outlines what is changing, why we made these decisions, and what you need to do before the end of the year.

Please read this in full. There are action items at the end that require your attention before **December 1, 2024**.

---

## The New Policy

**Meridian Software will operate under a Hybrid-First model.** Here is how it works:

**For Individual Contributors and Senior Individual Contributors**
You may work remotely up to four days per week. You are expected to be in the office — or at a designated co-working location if you are outside commuting distance — **a minimum of one day per week.** You and your direct manager will agree on which day that is, based on team needs.

**For Team Leads and Managers**
You are expected in the office **two days per week**, with at least one of those days aligned with your team's designated in-office day. This is about visibility and availability, not surveillance. Your teams need access to you.

**For Directors and Above**
A minimum of **three days per week** in the office applies to this group, reflecting the cross-functional coordination these roles require.

**Fully Remote Status**
Employees hired as fully remote prior to January 1, 2023, and those who received an approved remote designation during the 2022 restructuring retain their current arrangements. No change applies to you. If you are unsure of your designation, check your most recent offer letter or employment agreement, or contact People Operations.

**New Hires Going Forward**
All job postings will clearly specify the work location expectation for each role. We will hire for fully remote, hybrid, or on-site depending on team needs. This will be determined before a role is posted, not after someone accepts.

---

## Why We Are Making This Change

We want to be honest with you about what led here.

Over the past two years, we have operated with an informal, largely unwritten policy. In practice, that created inconsistency. Some managers required daily presence; others had no expectations at all. Employees in similar roles were being treated differently, which created frustration and, frankly, some quiet inequity based on who had the most assertive manager rather than what the work actually required.

At the same time, our own data — sprint velocity, project completion rates, and employee engagement scores — showed no meaningful difference in output between fully remote and hybrid teams. What did show up was a gap in collaboration quality for work that requires real-time creative problem-solving. Our engineering leads and product teams flagged this consistently. We take it seriously.

This policy tries to honor both realities: remote work is effective and employees value it deeply, *and* some things are better done in the same room. We are not pulling everyone back five days a week because that is not what the evidence supports, and it is not what we committed to when many of you joined us.

We are also aware that some of you relocated during the pandemic with our encouragement. This policy does not override individual agreements that were made in writing at that time.

---

## Office and Equipment

Our Portland headquarters will undergo a **workspace refresh** in November and December to accommodate the hybrid model. Assigned desks are being converted to a flexible reservation system for most of the floor. Directors and above will retain assigned spaces.

**Desk Reservation:** We are deploying a desk booking tool — details coming in a separate IT communication the week of October 21.

**Home Office Stipend:** The existing $75/month connectivity stipend continues. Employees who transition from fully remote to hybrid and need to update their home office setup may submit a one-time equipment request of up to **$300** through the People Ops portal. Requests must be submitted by March 31, 2025.

**Co-Working Locations:** If you live more than 50 miles from our Portland office, you may expense up to **$200/month** in co-working fees at an approved facility. A list of approved providers is available on the intranet.

---

## What You Need to Do Next

There are three required steps before **December 1, 2024.**

**1. Complete the Work Location Acknowledgment Form**
This form confirms you have read and understood the policy and identifies your work location category. It takes approximately five minutes. You will find it in Workday under *My Tasks*. This is required for all employees, including those with existing remote designations.

**2. Meet with Your Manager**
Between now and December 15, have a direct conversation with your manager to confirm your designated in-office day(s) and make any scheduling adjustments needed. Managers: please proactively schedule these conversations. Do not wait for your reports to come to you.

**3. Update Your Profile in Slack and the Directory**
Once your arrangement is confirmed, update your location and typical in-office days in your Slack profile and the company directory. This helps everyone coordinate and reduces unnecessary meeting friction.

---

## Questions and Exceptions

We expect questions. Here is where to direct them:

- **Policy interpretation questions:** Your manager or People Operations at people@meridiansoft.com
- **Individual accommodation requests** (medical, caregiving, or other): Contact People Operations directly and confidentially. These will be reviewed individually and are not subject to the standard timeline.
- **Technical or equipment questions:** IT Help Desk at the usual channels

We will also hold two **open Q&A sessions** via Zoom:
- **Thursday, October 24 at 10:00 AM PT**
- **Wednesday, October 30 at 3:00 PM PT**

Both sessions will be recorded and posted to the intranet within 48 hours for those who cannot attend live. Attendance is optional but encouraged.

---

## A Note on Trust

Policies like this one will always carry some risk of feeling like they are about distrust. That is not the intent. The 120 people at this company built something genuinely good over the past several years, much of it while working from kitchen tables and spare bedrooms. We have not forgotten that.

This policy is an attempt to be clearer and fairer, not more controlling. If it is applied in a way that feels punitive or inconsistent, please say so — to your manager, to People Ops, or directly to me. This document is the beginning of a conversation, not the end of one.

Thank you for your continued work and for the candor so many of you showed during this process.

— **Sarah Chen**
Chief People Officer
sarah.chen@meridiansoft.com | ext. 2201

---

*A full FAQ document will be posted to the intranet by October 18. The complete policy, including definitions and the exception request process, is available in the Employee Handbook, updated as of today's date.*

# INTERNAL MEMORANDUM

---

**TO:** All Warehouse Staff
**FROM:** Operations Management
**DATE:** November 10, 2025
**RE:** Holiday Season Shift Schedule Changes – Effective December 1, 2025

---

**PURPOSE**

As we approach our busiest time of year, we want to give you advance notice of upcoming changes to shift schedules across all warehouse departments. These adjustments are designed to meet increased order volume while ensuring fair and manageable workloads for every team member.

---

**SCHEDULE CHANGES**

- **Second Shift:** 1:00 PM – 9:30 PM (no change)
- **Third Shift:** 9:00 PM – 5:30 AM (previously 10:00 PM – 6:30 AM)

# Give the Bus Its Own Lane

Every morning, thousands of commuters sit trapped in the same ritual humiliation: a forty-minute crawl down a corridor that should take ten. Their cars inch forward. Their blood pressure climbs. And somewhere in the middle of that gridlock, a city bus carrying sixty people sits stuck in exactly the same jam, punished for doing the mathematically correct thing.

It is time to stop punishing the bus. It is time for dedicated bus lanes.

The argument against them is always the same, and it is always stated with great conviction by someone who has never actually done the math. "You're removing a lane of traffic," goes the objection. "You'll make congestion worse." This sounds reasonable until you consider what a lane of traffic actually carries. A single lane of mixed urban traffic moves somewhere between 600 and 1,600 vehicles per hour. A dedicated bus lane, running frequent service, can move upward of 8,000 people in that same space in the same time. The lane doesn't carry fewer people when you paint it red. It carries dramatically more.

Congestion is not caused by too little road space. It is caused by too many single-occupant vehicles competing for the space that exists. Adding capacity for cars — the approach we have tried for seventy years — reliably fills with new car traffic within a few years, a phenomenon so well-documented that transportation planners have a name for it: induced demand. You cannot build your way out of gridlock. But you can offer people a fast, reliable alternative that makes driving the less attractive option. That is precisely what a working bus rapid transit corridor does.

Consider what "fast and reliable" actually requires. Right now, the bus on this corridor stops at every red light, merges through the same bottlenecks, and gets stuck behind left-turning SUVs. It is slow not because buses are inherently slow, but because we have engineered slowness into the system by forcing buses to compete on equal terms with a mode of transport they were never designed to compete with. A dedicated lane, combined with transit signal priority — technology that holds a green light slightly longer as a bus approaches — transforms journey times. Cities that have implemented bus rapid transit corridors consistently report travel time reductions of 20 to 40 percent. Ridership rises. Operating costs per passenger fall. The system becomes self-reinforcing.

The equity argument is equally compelling, and it rarely receives the attention it deserves. The people most dependent on public transit are typically those with the least access to cars: lower-income workers, the elderly, people with disabilities, recent immigrants, students. When we design our roads exclusively around the automobile, we are designing them around a tool that a significant portion of the city's population cannot use or cannot afford. Dedicating a lane to the bus is not a gift to transit riders at the expense of drivers. It is an acknowledgment that the people in that bus have exactly as much right to move efficiently through the city as anyone else — more right, arguably, given how little road space they collectively consume.

Opponents will raise the issue of business access, and that concern deserves a serious answer. Evidence from cities that have implemented bus lanes — London, Bogotá, Brisbane, Los Angeles — consistently shows that retail corridors with high-quality transit access perform as well or better than comparable car-oriented corridors. Customers who arrive by bus spend more time and, over the course of a week, often as much money as those who arrive by car. Removing the friction of transit access tends to increase foot traffic, not diminish it.

None of this requires pretending the transition is painless. Some drivers will have a worse commute in the short term. Some loading and parking arrangements will need to change. Political will is required, and political will is exactly what these proposals tend to encounter resistance to expending. It is much easier to do nothing, to let the gridlock calcify, to keep promising studies and consultations and pilot programs that quietly expire.

But doing nothing has a cost too. It is just a cost distributed invisibly — in lost hours, in diesel emissions, in the slow economic suffocation of a city that cannot move its people efficiently, in the daily indignity handed to the sixty people on that bus while the corridor chokes around them.

Give the bus its own lane. Count the people, not the vehicles. Build the city around movement, not around cars.

The math has been done. The only question is whether we have the nerve to act on it.

# Bring Back the Corner Store: Why Our Neighborhoods Need Small Businesses on Their Blocks

There is a particular kind of despair that sets in when you realize you have run out of milk and the nearest grocery store is a twenty-minute drive away. For millions of Americans living in strictly zoned residential neighborhoods, this is not a hypothetical inconvenience — it is Tuesday. Meanwhile, the solution is staring us in the face, blocked only by outdated zoning codes and a misplaced fear of change: the corner store.

It is time for residential neighborhoods across the country to welcome small corner stores back into their blocks. The arguments for doing so are practical, social, environmental, and economic. The arguments against them, when examined honestly, are mostly rooted in anxiety rather than evidence.

## We Have Engineered Inconvenience Into Our Communities

For most of human history, people lived within walking distance of food, medicine, and basic goods. The corner store, the neighborhood market, the bodega — these were not exceptions to residential life. They were woven into it. Then came post-war zoning policy, which rigidly separated commercial from residential use under the theory that mixing them created nuisance and blight. Decades later, we are left with neighborhoods where you cannot buy a loaf of bread without getting into a car.

This is not progress. This is a policy failure masquerading as neighborhood protection.

Strict single-use zoning has contributed directly to food deserts, where low-income residents — often without reliable vehicle access — struggle to reach affordable groceries. It has increased car dependency, adding to traffic, emissions, and the cost of living. It has hollowed out the social fabric of neighborhoods by eliminating the casual, daily points of contact between residents. Allowing small corner stores does not create these problems. It begins to solve them.

## The Fears Are Largely Unfounded

Opponents of mixed-use zoning in residential areas typically raise a predictable set of concerns: increased traffic, noise, loitering, crime, and declining property values. These concerns deserve direct answers rather than dismissal.

Traffic and parking anxiety is perhaps the most common objection. But a well-placed corner store actually *reduces* vehicle trips in a neighborhood by allowing residents to walk or bike for everyday needs rather than drive to a distant commercial strip. Studies of walkable, mixed-use neighborhoods consistently show lower per-capita vehicle miles traveled. A small store draws foot traffic, not car traffic.

As for crime and loitering: this argument has long been used to keep commercial activity out of residential areas, and the evidence does not support it. In fact, active street life — the kind generated by a neighborhood store with steady foot traffic — is one of the most powerful deterrents to crime that urban planners have identified. Jane Jacobs called it "eyes on the street." A dark, empty block is far more inviting to criminal activity than one with a lit storefront and a steady stream of neighbors stopping in for coffee.

Property values are the most emotionally charged concern, and the most interestingly wrong one. Research on walkability consistently finds that homes in neighborhoods with accessible amenities, including small retail, command price premiums over car-dependent equivalents. People pay more to live where they can walk to things. Allowing corner stores does not threaten residential property values — in many cases, it enhances them.

## The Social Case Is Compelling

There is something that numbers alone cannot fully capture, and that is the role the corner store plays in community life. The bodega owner who knows your name, the shop where neighbors run into each other on a Saturday morning, the place where a newcomer to the block first feels like a part of something — these are not trivial amenities. They are the connective tissue of neighborhood identity.

Robert Putnam and others have written extensively about the decline of social capital in American life. We bowl alone. We do not know our neighbors. We are lonely in ways that have measurable consequences for physical and mental health. Zoning policy is not the only cause of this, but it is a contributing one. When we design neighborhoods with no reason to leave your car or your home except to commute, we should not be surprised when people feel disconnected.

The corner store is a humble but powerful antidote. It creates a shared space with low barriers to entry — you buy a newspaper, you exchange a few words, you belong a little more to where you live.

## Practical Safeguards Are Readily Available

None of this requires an abandonment of all standards. A city or county that wants to permit corner stores in residential neighborhoods can do so with thoughtful conditions: limits on square footage, restrictions on hours of operation, design standards that ensure buildings complement the neighborhood aesthetic, requirements for adequate lighting and waste management. These are reasonable regulations, not obstacles.

Many cities have already moved in this direction with encouraging results. Minneapolis eliminated single-family-only zoning entirely. Cities across Europe have long maintained the tradition of the neighborhood shop on the residential street. Incremental rezoning — allowing small-scale retail by right on certain residential corners — is a measured, low-risk way to test and expand the approach.

## The Neighborhood We Say We Want

Ask most people to describe their ideal neighborhood, and they will describe something with a coffee shop they can walk to, a market nearby, a sense of life on the streets. Then ask them if they support allowing a small store to open on the corner of their block, and some will hesitate. This gap between what we want and what we will permit must be closed.

Our neighborhoods are not museums. They are living places, and living places need the amenities of daily life within reach. The corner store is not a threat to the residential neighborhood. It is one of its oldest and most essential features.

Let it come back.

# Keep the Lights On: Why Our Libraries Must Stay Open Later

Every evening at six o'clock, something quietly unfortunate happens across our city. The library doors close, the lights go dark, and thousands of residents are left without one of the most valuable public resources their tax dollars provide. It is time to change that. Our city library system should extend its evening hours — and the case for doing so is both urgent and obvious.

## The People Who Need It Most Are Working During the Day

Libraries are often described as great equalizers, places where a student from a struggling household has access to the same books, computers, and research tools as anyone else. But that promise rings hollow when the library closes before many working families get home.

Consider who fills library chairs on a typical weekday afternoon: retirees, young children with stay-at-home parents, and college students with flexible schedules. These are not the people who need the library most desperately — or rather, they are not the *only* people who do. The overnight shift nurse, the warehouse worker pulling a nine-to-five, the single parent who picks up children from day care at 5:30 — these residents pay taxes too. Closing at six means the library effectively belongs to people who already have the most flexibility. That is a quiet form of inequality we rarely examine directly, and it deserves examination.

## Students Cannot Afford to Lose the Evening Hours

Walk through any public library on a Tuesday afternoon and you will find teenagers bent over homework at communal tables. Many of them are there not because their own homes are unwelcoming, but because those homes lack reliable internet, a quiet space, or the simple psychological separation that makes studying possible. When the library closes at six, those students are pushed back into environments that make academic success harder.

College and university students fare no better. Evening is often the only time they can access specialized databases, printing services, or quiet study rooms after a full day of classes and part-time work. A library that closes at six is, to many students, barely open at all. We speak endlessly about closing the educational achievement gap. Extending library hours is one of the most straightforward, affordable steps we can take toward that goal.

## The Digital Divide Is Real and It Has Office Hours

We frequently discuss the digital divide in abstract terms, but it has a very specific daily schedule. For low-income residents who rely on library computers and broadband internet, that divide slams shut every evening when the building locks up. Job applications, telehealth appointments, online coursework, government benefits enrollment — these tasks increasingly happen online, and they do not always fit neatly between nine and six.

A 2019 Pew Research Center survey found that roughly one in five American adults does not have home broadband service, a figure that climbs sharply among lower-income households. Public libraries represent their primary access point to the connected world. Restricting that access to daytime hours is not a neutral logistical decision. It is a policy choice that disadvantages people who are already disadvantaged.

## Evening Hours Make Libraries Safer Centers of Community

There is a practical, civic argument that goes beyond individual users. Libraries that stay open in the evening become anchors of safety and community in neighborhoods that otherwise have very few. A well-lit, staffed public space where teenagers can gather, where adults can take English-language classes, where job seekers can connect with workforce development programs — this is not a luxury. It is infrastructure.

Cities that have experimented with extended library hours have frequently reported positive effects on foot traffic in surrounding commercial areas, reduced loitering and associated complaints, and greater community attachment to the institution. When a library is open, it says something to a neighborhood: *someone is here, this place belongs to you, you are welcome.* That message is worth staying open an extra two or three hours to send.

## The Cost Objection Deserves an Honest Answer

Critics will raise the obvious concern: extending hours costs money. Staffing, utilities, and security for additional evening hours represent a real budget line. This objection deserves a direct response rather than dismissal.

First, the cost is more modest than opponents suggest. Many branches could extend hours on a rotating basis, ensuring coverage without requiring every location to add identical shifts. Partnerships with library science graduate programs can provide cost-effective staffing. Second, the right comparison is not "extended hours versus nothing" but "extended hours versus the cost of the problems we fail to address" — underemployment, educational underachievement, digital exclusion. Libraries are among the most cost-efficient public investments a city makes. The return on a few additional evening hours is disproportionately high.

Finally, this is a matter of political will as much as budgetary math. Cities find money for what they decide to prioritize. The question is whether we genuinely believe the library belongs to all residents or primarily to those whose schedules are already comfortable.

## A Simple Act of Inclusion

Keeping libraries open until eight or nine o'clock in the evening is not a radical proposal. It is a practical act of inclusion — an acknowledgment that public services should serve the public as it actually exists, not as we imagine it. It says that a working mother's need for a quiet place to help her children with homework at seven p.m. matters just as much as anyone else's afternoon convenience.

The library is one of democracy's finest inventions: free, open, and built on the conviction that knowledge belongs to everyone. Let us make sure the hours of operation actually reflect that conviction.

# The Clock and the Contract

The foreman's name was Darnell, and the first thing he told me was to slow down.

I had just finished stacking forty-three boxes in the time it was supposed to take me to stack twenty, and I felt the familiar voltage of having impressed someone. I was twenty-four years old, recently arrived at a warehouse job in a mid-sized city where I knew nobody, and I had spent the previous six years learning that the only way to survive work was to be visibly faster, visibly more eager, visibly more willing than everyone around you. I had worked retail. I had waited tables. I had temp-staffed through three different light-industrial facilities where supervisors circled the floor like birds of prey looking for someone standing still. Speed was the only currency I knew.

Darnell—heavyset, deliberate, wearing a Steelworkers local pin on his vest that I didn't yet understand—looked at my forty-three boxes and said, "You're going to hurt yourself, and then you're going to hurt the rest of us. Slow down."

I thought he was being lazy. That is the honest thing to say. I thought this was a union shop and therefore a place where people got away with things, where mediocrity was protected from consequence, where the guys who'd been there longest coasted while the new people picked up the slack. I had absorbed this idea from somewhere—from the cultural air, probably, from a decade of news cycles and a stepfather who ran a small landscaping business and talked about unions the way some people talk about the weather: a force that happened to you and ruined things. I was ready to be disappointed.

What Darnell was actually telling me—and what I would not understand for another three weeks, when I finally read the contract—was that the pace had been negotiated. The production standards existed in writing, had been argued over and agreed upon by men and women who worked this floor and knew what a human body could sustain over eight hours, over forty hours, over thirty years. When I raced ahead of the standard, I was not showing initiative. I was handing management a number they could use to revise expectations upward for everyone. I was, without knowing it, bargaining against my coworkers.

This was the first new idea about work I had encountered in my life.

---

The break room smelled like burned coffee and the industrial cleaner that came up through the concrete floor on cold mornings. There was a bulletin board with the contract tacked to it—the actual contract, forty-two pages, available for anyone to read—and on my lunch break during that first week I sat and paged through it the way you might read a document in a dream, recognizing the language without fully grasping what you were seeing. Wages. Grievance procedures. A process for disputing discipline that involved representation, that involved steps, that involved the company having to justify itself in writing.

At every previous job, discipline had been a conversation in a back office that you had no right to record, no right to contest, and no guarantee anyone would remember accurately. You were let go for reasons that could be framed as anything. I once watched a coworker at a restaurant lose her job three weeks after filing a complaint about a manager, and the stated reason was that she'd comped a dessert without authorization. That may even have been true—but there was no way to know what the real reason was, and no mechanism to find out, and no one to ask.

The contract had a word for what happened to her: retaliation. And it had a procedure that followed from that word. The word had weight because it was written down, because other people had signed it, because it existed independently of whoever was angry at you on a particular Tuesday.

I don't want to make this sound like I discovered utopia in a warehouse outside the city on a gray November morning. There were plenty of grievances that went nowhere, Darnell told me later. There were arbitrations lost. There were supervisors who found legal ways to make life difficult for people they didn't like. The contract was a floor, not a ceiling, and you could still fall through the cracks between the words.

But a floor. The concept of a floor.

---

At the end of my first week, there was a union meeting in the break room after the shift. Maybe thirty people crowded in, some standing against the walls. I noticed that nobody was performing. Nobody was trying to look good for management, because management wasn't there. The local president, a woman named Cecilia who had worked the floor for nineteen years and had arms like bridge cables, stood at the front with a legal pad and ran through agenda items in the flat tone of someone who had done this many times and trusted the people she was talking to.

There was a dispute about mandatory overtime procedures. Someone had a concern about a new hoist that they thought was a safety issue. There was a vote—an actual vote, with hands, with a count—on whether to file a formal grievance about a scheduling change that the company had implemented without the required notice.

The vote passed. The grievance would be filed.

I sat in the back and felt something that I can only describe, even now, as grief, though that sounds wrong. It was grief for the years I had spent running fast and staying quiet, believing that the only available relationship between a worker and a workplace was a private one—your individual effort exchanged for individual approval, with no accumulation, no record, no recourse. I had believed, without examining the belief, that this was simply the nature of work. That the anxiety was structural, immovable. That the feeling of being replaceable was just an accurate perception of reality.

I was twenty-four years old and I had never seen thirty people vote on anything that affected their own lives.

---

I stayed in that job for two years before I left to go back to school, and in those two years I thought frequently about what it was costing people who would never have it. The friend who was managing a coffee shop chain and sleeping four hours a night and couldn't take a sick day without the schedule collapsing, who was grateful for her job because she'd had worse ones. The cousin who'd been fired by text message and spent two months believing he had somehow deserved it. The sense—widespread as oxygen in the places I'd come from—that to ask for anything was to risk everything, and so you asked for nothing, and you ran fast, and you told yourself this was strength.

Darnell's slowdown was not laziness. It was the product of a negotiation, and the negotiation was the product of people deciding that the terms of their labor were not beyond discussion, were not simply handed down from the nature of things. Someone before him had been willing to be uncomfortable, to organize, to put their name on a demand. The contract on the bulletin board was not magic. It was the residue of conflict, the record of a disagreement that had been won.

I think about the bulletin board sometimes, still. I think about the particular quality of being able to walk up to a wall and read the terms of your own situation—dry, legal, binding—and know that those terms were not merely the preference of whoever happened to be in charge that day.

Work is where most of us spend most of our lives. We move through it with whatever map we inherited, and most of us inherited a map that told us it was a wilderness, uncharted, where only individual cunning kept you alive. Some places have different maps. Some places have bulletin boards.

I think everyone deserves to slow down.

# The 12:47

The first thing you learn about a late-night bus is that it has its own constitution, its own social contract, entirely different from the one governing the 8 a.m. version of the same route. The 8 a.m. is performance. Everyone performing alertness, purpose, destination. The 12:47 is something closer to confession.

I rode it for a year. Route 14, the long diagonal that cuts from downtown across the river and empties out in the flatlands near the highway, where I lived in a second-floor apartment above a dog groomer. The smell of wet dog shampoo rose through my floors on weekend mornings. I thought I hated it. I'm not sure anymore.

I took the 12:47 because I worked late at a restaurant, the kind of place where closing meant mopping at eleven and counting register cash at midnight and standing outside in the alley afterward with the other servers, postponing the loneliness of going home. I was twenty-nine and had recently stopped being in love with someone, or had recently admitted I'd stopped, which felt like two different losses arriving in the same envelope.

The driver that fall was a man I came to think of as Gerald, though I never knew his name. Heavyset, with reading glasses pushed up on his forehead, the kind of face that has settled into itself completely. He never said good evening and he never said watch your step and I respected him enormously for it. He simply opened the door. That was the deal. You got on, you paid, the door closed. The bus moved. These are the fundamentals. Gerald understood the fundamentals.

---

By November I had memorized the regulars.

There was the woman I called the Judge. She was perhaps sixty, wore a wool coat even in mild weather, and read thick paperback novels with the covers bent all the way back, a habit that would have horrified me in any other context but which on the 12:47 seemed like survival strategy, a way of taking up necessary psychic space. She got on at the stop near the hospital and off near the community college. I constructed entire life histories for her. Night-shift administrative work. An adult son she worried about. Crossword puzzles completed in pen. I was probably wrong about everything.

There was a young man who carried a guitar case and always sat in the very back, center seat, as though he were about to give a concert to the rear window. He smelled of cigarettes and something else, cedar maybe, and he stared at his phone with an expression of absolute devastation every single time. I don't know if it was the same devastation or new devastation delivered nightly. The latter seemed more plausible. He was young enough for that.

There was a pair of women who got on together three stops after me, always laughing when they boarded and always asleep within five minutes, their heads migrating toward each other's shoulders with a slow inevitability that felt like watching time-lapse footage of something tender. I never saw them awake on the bus. They arrived in laughter and departed in sleep and I envied them both things.

And there was the bus itself. The particular bus, with its ripped seat in the fourth row that had been ripped in exactly the same L-shape every night for months, hemorrhaging yellow foam. The overhead light at the back that flickered when the bus went over a certain seam in the road near the bridge, so that for three seconds the back of the bus strobed like a failing disco. The doors that sighed when they opened, a genuine exhalation, something almost human in it.

---

In December I started bringing books. Before that I had been bringing my phone, doing what everyone does on their phone, which is to say I had been performing the act of being occupied. The book felt different. The book felt like what the Judge was doing, creating a perimeter. I read badly at first, the words not cohering, my brain still metabolizing the restaurant noise, the particular exhaustion of being relentlessly pleasant to strangers. But by the third week of December I had found my rhythm. Board the bus. Find a seat. Open the book. Read until the bridge. Look up at the strobe. Read until my stop.

I read six books that winter. I remember almost none of their contents. What I remember is reading them.

One night in January — the coldest night of the year, the kind of cold that makes the city look like a photograph of itself, everything still and high-contrast — there were only four of us on the bus. Me, the Judge, the guitar player, and a man I'd never seen before who sat near the front and talked quietly into his phone in a language I didn't recognize, his voice rising and falling in a pattern that sounded like worry and then reassurance and then worry again. Gerald drove. The heater ran. The windows fogged from the inside, the city going soft and watercolor beyond the glass, and I had the sudden, piercing sensation that this was enough. That this sealed tube of warmth and strangers moving through the cold dark was not a means to anywhere but was, for twenty-two minutes, a complete thing.

I didn't do anything with that feeling. I was twenty-nine. I wrote it down later on a receipt I found in my jacket pocket, in handwriting so small and cramped it took me a week to read it back.

---

Spring changed the roster. The Judge disappeared in April and I mourned her with a disproportionate intensity that I recognized, even then, as displacement. The guitar player started getting off two stops earlier, then stopped appearing altogether. New people arrived. A teenager with a fast-food uniform. An older man with a small wheeled suitcase, appearing every Friday like punctuation.

Gerald was replaced by a woman who said good evening to everyone, brisk and genuine, and I liked her but it took me until May to stop resenting her for not being Gerald.

I began to notice that I was doing something on the bus that I wasn't doing anywhere else, which was simply sitting with what the day had been. No resolution, no narrative arc imposed on it. Just the bus moving and downtown receding and the river going past with its bridges lit above it, and whatever had happened that day happening again quietly inside me without any obligation to conclude. My therapist, whom I saw on Tuesday mornings, would have called this processing. On the bus I just called it riding.

The person I had stopped loving texted me twice that spring. I read both texts on the bus. I answered one of them. I don't know now if I made the right choice but I know that I made it in the right place, under the flickering light near the bridge, the city blurring past.

---

There's something the bus knows that the rest of transit infrastructure doesn't quite know. The subway is too fast and too subterranean; it annihilates the city rather than moving through it. The taxi and the rideshare are too private, too tailored to you specifically, the route adjusted for your singular address. But the bus is on its own schedule, its own geography. You accommodate the bus. You walk to where it goes. You wait at its hour. You share it with whoever else has arranged their life around these same coordinates, and for the length of the route you are a temporary collective, held together by nothing but direction and time.

I didn't know any of their names. The Judge, the guitar player, the sleeping women, Gerald, not-Gerald. I knew their coats. I knew their postures. I knew exactly how the guitar player held his phone and how the Judge turned a page and how the sleeping women's heads found each other with that slow, certain gravity. I knew them the way you know the furniture of a room you've lived in long enough to stop seeing: completely, and without effort, and mostly in the dark.

---

The last time I took the 12:47 was in October, almost exactly a year after I'd started. I was leaving the job. I had given notice two weeks earlier, and someone had bought me a bottle of wine and someone else had given me a card signed by people I'd worked beside for a year and a half, and I had stood in the alley afterward alone, not postponing anything, just standing there while the moment became a former moment.

On the bus, I didn't read. I just sat and watched the city do its late-night thing through the fogged window. A woman I'd never seen before was sitting in the fourth row, settling herself right into the ripped seat as though it were nothing, as though she'd never known it any other way. The bridge came. The lights stuttered. I thought about the receipt in my jacket pocket from January, how small I'd had to make the words to fit.

The bus reached my stop. I gathered my jacket. Gerald — it was Gerald again, he'd come back in August, still wearing the reading glasses pushed up on his forehead — opened the door and I stepped down into air that smelled like October and nearby river water and the particular electrical smell of late-night streets.

I stood on the curb and the door sighed shut and the bus pulled away, and I watched its lights diminish down the long straight road.

Then I walked home. The dog grooming smell met me on the stairs. I put the bottle of wine on the counter and sat at the kitchen table without turning on the overhead light, just the streetlight coming through the window, and I let the year be exactly as long as it had been, which was, all things considered, not long at all.

# The Deep End of Everything

The summer I was nine, I learned that a town is not the place it pretends to be. I learned this at the Ridgecrest Municipal Pool, which smelled of chlorine and sunscreen and something underneath both of those things — something older, harder to name, that I would spend decades trying to identify before I finally understood it was the smell of a line being held.

The pool was on Garfield Street, three blocks from the elementary school, and it cost seventy-five cents to get in. That was the price for everyone — the sign said so right there at the window, black letters on a white board, seventy-five cents, and there was something about seeing it written down that made you feel like fairness was being enforced by the universe itself. Seventy-five cents. Same for everybody.

What the sign did not mention was that the pool opened at ten in the morning and closed at six in the evening, and that most of the adults in the west part of town — the part where the houses had aluminum siding where vinyl hadn't replaced it, and where the fathers worked the early shift at the distribution center and the mothers worked the lunch shift at the diner on Route 9 — did not get off work until five-thirty at the earliest. So the pool was technically available to everyone in the same way that a museum is technically available to everyone: equal in theory, rationed by circumstance.

I lived on the east side, which is to say I lived with my mother in a house that had a dishwasher and two bathrooms, and my father was an accountant with an office on the second floor of the Merchants Exchange Building downtown. We were not rich people — my parents were careful to remind me of this, and I believed them, though I understand now that careful people who have to remind their children they are not rich are, in fact, the particular kind of comfortable that gets to feel virtuous for not being more so. We went to the pool on Tuesday and Thursday afternoons, and my mother packed a bag with SPF 30 and a cooler with sliced apples and juice boxes, and we spread our towels near the deep end, under the good shade of the two oak trees that arched over the south fence.

The other children I knew were mostly from the east side too. We held the pool in those afternoon hours the way you hold a room when you arrive first and set your things down.

The west-side kids arrived later. They came in groups, on bikes or on foot, and they arrived louder than we did — not because they were different children but because they were arriving at something they had earned by waiting, by existing past five o'clock with enough coins in their pockets. They found us already settled, already dry, already comfortable in the language of the place. And I noticed even then, at nine years old, that the lifeguards — both of them teenage boys from the east side, one of them my neighbor's son — watched different ends of the pool depending on which children were in them.

I want to be careful not to reduce this to a simple story, because it was not simple, and because the people in it were not symbols. There was a girl from the west side named Destiny who could do a backflip off the diving board at age eight and who had a laugh that seemed to reorganize the air around it. There was a boy named Marcus who was afraid of the deep end and who held the wall for an entire summer before one evening in late July when he let go and swam across to the other side and came up gasping and grinning, and his older sister screamed from the bleachers like he had just done something biblical. I was there for that. I remember the particular quality of the light that evening, going gold and long across the water. I remember thinking: okay, this place can hold that.

But I also remember the afternoon a man from the west side came to pick up his daughter and was told by one of the lifeguards — my neighbor's son, who I knew by name, whose dog I had petted a hundred times — that adult men were not allowed in the pool area after five unless they were accompanying children who were already registered for the day. This was delivered with a tone that was not a tone the lifeguard used with the east-side fathers. The man from the west side looked at the lifeguard for a moment. Then he looked past him at his daughter, who was in the shallow end, who had seen. He said something quiet and walked to the gate, and his daughter came to him.

I watched this from my towel. I was nine, and I did not have the language for what I was watching, but my body understood something — a tightening in the chest, a heat that had nothing to do with the sun. I looked at my mother. She was reading a magazine. The afternoon went on.

That is the thing about a public pool in a small American town: it surfaces, visibly and literally, the arrangements a place has quietly made with itself. The democracy of water — everyone wet, everyone cold when the shadow of a cloud passes — coexists with the bureaucracy of hours, of rules selectively applied, of the invisible math that determines who gets to feel at ease. A pool is a public space, which means it belongs to everyone in the civic sense, the legal sense, the posted-sign sense. And then you go there and watch a few summer afternoons unspool, and you understand what belonging actually means, which is not the same thing.

I went back to Ridgecrest fifteen years later, the summer my mother was in the hospital and I was staying at the house. The pool was still there. The price had gone up to three dollars. The oak trees were gone — a fungus, someone told me, had taken them in the nineties — and the south side of the deck was hot with full sun all afternoon now. The sign was the same: three dollars, same for everybody.

I didn't go in. I stood at the fence for a while and watched the children in the water — all of them, for those suspended minutes, equally submerged, equally buoyant, the afternoon light doing its ancient work on the surface of the pool, breaking and reassembling, breaking and reassembling. A girl launched herself off the board and was briefly, perfectly airborne, and the crowd on the deck made that involuntary sound — half gasp, half cheer — that a body in flight always draws from other bodies.

For one moment, it looked like the thing the sign promised.

Then I walked back to my car, to my mother, to the accumulated knowledge of what I'd learned in that water and at its edges — that a town will show you its actual self not in its stated principles but in the thousand quiet transactions of an ordinary afternoon. That the deep end and the shallow end have always been metaphor as much as geography. That the price of admission is rarely what it says on the sign.

# The Same Water, Every Day

## A Portrait of Captain Maren Solås

The ferry leaves at 6:15, and Maren Solås is already at the wheel by six.

She does not arrive early out of anxiety. She arrives early because the harbor at Kvåsund looks different at six than it does at any other hour, and after thirty-one years she has not stopped wanting to see it. The light comes low off the Kattegat and turns the chop between the two headlands into something that resembles hammered copper, and she stands in the wheelhouse with her coffee and watches it happen the way other people watch fire. Then the gangway begins to creak and shift with the weight of the first passengers, and the moment passes, and she goes to work.

The route between Kvåsund and Bredvik is eleven nautical miles. On a calm day it takes forty-seven minutes. On a bad day—and there are bad days, whole November weeks when the strait turns white and the passengers grip their armrests and look straight ahead—it can take over an hour. Maren has run the crossing somewhere in the neighborhood of twenty-two thousand times. She does not say this to impress anyone. She says it the way a baker might mention how many loaves. It is simply what the math comes out to.

She is fifty-eight years old, broad through the shoulders, with gray hair she keeps in a practical braid and hands that seem slightly too large for her frame, which her youngest daughter once told her looked like a fisherman's hands. Maren said she'd take that as a compliment. She took the job at twenty-seven, after a decade crewing on supply vessels in the North Sea, when her father's health declined and she wanted to be closer to the coast where she'd grown up. In those early years she sometimes thought she would stay for two seasons and then go back out. She is still here.

---

What changes on a route you know by heart is not the route. It is everything else.

The MF *Bredvik* that Maren runs today is the third vessel to carry that name in her service. The first two were retired, parts sold off or scrapped, and she attended both send-offs with a feeling she describes as "something like grief, but quieter." The current *Bredvik* is a modern double-ended ro-ro ferry, low-slung and unglamorous, built in 2009 in a Polish yard. It carries up to thirty-two vehicles and two hundred and forty passengers. It has a warm, faintly diesel-smelling passenger cabin that Maren has walked through so many thousands of times she can navigate it in total darkness without touching a wall, which she once had to do during a power interruption in 2017 and did without drama.

The crossing connects two towns that most people between them would say are nothing alike. Kvåsund has a consolidated school, a supermarket, a harbor district that once processed cod by the ton and now processes tourists in the same spirit. Bredvik is quieter, older in feeling, its economic center shifted inland years ago toward a regional logistics hub that has nothing to do with the sea. The ferry is still the most direct link between them. The bridge that has been proposed, debated, costed, and abandoned happens every twelve years or so like a bad recurring dream. Maren does not publicly oppose the bridge. She does not need to. The economics have always killed it before the politics could.

Her passengers are, after three decades, a kind of extended and involuntary community. She knows who commutes, who visits, who returns from treatment at the regional hospital in Bredvik on Thursdays with a particular careful expression. She knows the farmers who bring livestock trailers across in the early morning and the contractor who has been renovating the same house in Kvåsund since 2014 and jokes, every time, that he'll be done by summer. She does not interfere in their lives, but she is present at the edges of most of them, a steady thing they pass through twice a day. Several people have told her that they find the crossing calming. One man—a retired teacher named Gunnar Holt who crossed every Tuesday for fifteen years to visit his sister—once told her that the sound of the engine changing pitch as they came into the Bredvik dock was the most reliable sound in his life. She has thought about that more than she expected to.

---

There is a particular quality of attention that comes from knowing something deeply.

Most pilots and captains speak of their routes and corridors in terms of hazards and protocols, the language of risk management. Maren speaks in those terms when she is speaking officially. Unofficially she speaks of the strait the way a person speaks of a difficult relative: with intimacy and without illusion. She knows where the current pulls hard along the eastern skerries in ebb tide. She knows the precise formation of rock, just below the surface at the Kvåsund approaches, that the charts mark conservatively and that she has privately and more precisely mapped through decades of observation. She knows what the color of the water at the midway point predicts about the wind in the next two hours with a reliability that she says beats the meteorological service in March and April by a significant margin. The service, when she once mentioned this, did not seem charmed.

She has evacuated the ferry twice. On a Tuesday morning in 2003, when a vehicle fire below decks required putting everyone in the water—all passengers recovered, no fatalities, a regional maritime commendation that lives in a drawer somewhere. And once, in 2011, when structural ice in an early freeze made the Bredvik dock temporarily impassable and she made the decision to anchor and wait rather than attempt a berth that she judged unsafe, holding two hundred and ten passengers in the strait for four hours while the situation was assessed. She was second-guessed afterward. She was not wrong.

She has also brought people into the world on the crossing. Twice. The first time, in 1998, she handed the wheel to her first officer and sat on the passenger cabin floor with a woman named Astrid Engvik for twenty-two minutes while her husband stood nearby in the condition of a man whose entire knowledge base had become suddenly useless. The child, a girl, was named after the vessel. The second time, in 2014, she was less needed—a midwife was among the passengers—and she stayed at the wheel as protocol required, but she slowed the engine slightly and came into Kvåsund as gently as she knew how.

---

When people ask if she ever gets bored—and they ask—she answers the question they meant to ask rather than the one they asked.

"You can't be bored if you're paying attention," she says. "And you always have to be paying attention."

She is not performing contentment. She has had years she found difficult: a period in the mid-2000s when a licensing dispute with the new regional transport authority felt designed to push out older independent operators; a winter after her mother died when the crossing seemed, for the first time, merely mechanical and gray. She knows what it is to stand at the wheel with no feeling at all, which is different from peace.

But she stayed. And the reasons she stayed are below language, or at least below the language she reaches for easily. She talks around them: the way she knows this particular stretch of water, the way the job has a shape she fits inside. She says that she has a hard time imagining what it would mean to become good at something else at this point in her life, and that she is not sure she would want to find out.

She docks at 6:58 and loads the return crossing at 7:20, and the day proceeds from there in its increments, and the light changes above the strait the way it always has and always does, and the water is never exactly the same water twice, and she pays attention.

# The Weight of Every Case: A Public Defender's Day

The file folders are stacked in two leaning towers on the corner of Maya Reyes's desk, and she has already apologized for them twice before sitting down. It is 7:42 in the morning. Court starts at nine.

"I used to organize them alphabetically," she says, pulling one from the middle of a stack with the practiced confidence of someone who has learned which cards the tower will forgive. "Now I organize them by who's incarcerated. Those people are on a clock."

Reyes is 34 and has worked as a public defender in the Allegheny County Public Defender's Office — Pittsburgh, population 300,000, neither the largest nor smallest stage for this kind of work — for six years. She currently carries 112 open cases. The American Bar Association recommends that a public defender handle no more than 150 felonies per year. Reyes passed that number in August, and it is November.

---

Public defenders occupy one of the stranger positions in American civic life. They are constitutionally guaranteed — *Gideon v. Wainwright*, 1963, established that the Sixth Amendment requires the state to provide counsel to defendants who cannot afford one — and chronically underfunded. They are often the most experienced attorneys in a courtroom, handling more trials in a year than many private lawyers see in a decade, and they are routinely dismissed, in ways both subtle and overt, as the legal system's second tier.

Reyes does not spend much energy on the perception. She has 112 cases.

"People ask me how I do it," she says. "I think they're asking an existential question, like how do I live with myself. But the actual answer is logistical. You triage. You build systems. You sleep when you can."

Her systems are visible throughout the office, a floor of a county building with fluorescent lighting and carpet that might once have been green. She has a color-coded calendar on one monitor, a client-tracking spreadsheet on the other. She keeps a notebook with the names of incarcerated clients and their bail amounts — not because she doesn't know the numbers, but because writing them down, she says, reminds her they're people waiting on her, not files waiting on her.

---

The morning's docket has four of her clients on it. One is a drug possession case she expects to resolve with a plea — a young man, 22, first offense, and she spent two days negotiating a diversion program that will keep a conviction off his record. She explains this without pride, the way a plumber explains fixing a pipe. It is what needed to happen.

Another case she is less settled about. Her client is a 47-year-old man charged with aggravated assault after a fight outside a bar. Reyes believes the charge is overreaching — the complaining witness has a history with her client that the police report omits, and the surveillance footage, which she requested in September, has still not been fully disclosed by the prosecution.

"In a private firm, someone would have filed three motions and made two phone calls and that footage would have materialized," she says, not bitterly, as observation. "I filed the motion. The calls are harder when you're calling about eleven different things the same week."

She does not blame prosecutors in general, she is careful to say. Several of the ADAs she appears against regularly are professional and fair. The problem is structural — a funding imbalance that means the office investigating and charging a crime has more resources than the office defending against it. Pittsburgh is not unusual in this. A 2022 report from the Brennan Center for Justice found that public defenders in most jurisdictions are funded at roughly half the rate of prosecutors' offices.

---

By 10:15 the diversion agreement is signed. Her client shakes her hand and she holds it for a moment — she is deliberate about this, she says later, about letting people feel that the interaction was real — and then he is gone and she is already looking at her phone, where an investigator has texted about a witness in a robbery case set for trial in three weeks.

Between the morning session and the afternoon calendar, she eats a sandwich at her desk and takes a call from a client's mother, who wants to understand why the case is taking so long. Reyes explains, again, the mechanics of discovery and scheduling. She is patient. She has had this conversation many hundreds of times, and she knows it is never really about discovery. It is about a family watching someone they love move through a system that seems to operate on its own clock, indifferent to theirs.

"I got into this because of a professor who said public defense was where constitutional law actually lives," she says after she hangs up. "Like, the Bill of Rights isn't abstract here. Every day I'm touching the Fourth Amendment, the Sixth Amendment. It matters what I do. That's still true. I haven't talked myself out of that."

She pauses.

"But I also know that last week I had a client plead guilty to something I think we could have fought, and I think part of why he agreed to the plea is that he could see how stretched I was. He didn't want to be a burden. A 19-year-old kid didn't want to burden his public defender." She straightens some papers. "That's the thing I carry."

---

The afternoon brings a bail hearing for a client who has been in the county jail for 61 days awaiting trial on a theft charge. The original bail was set at $10,000 — a figure her client, who works part-time at a warehouse, cannot meet. Reyes has filed for reduction. She presents the judge with documentation: her client's employment record, a letter from his landlord, his lack of prior convictions, the fact that he appeared for every required appointment before his arrest.

The judge reduces bail to $2,500. Her client, watching by video from the jail, looks like a man who has received news he was afraid to hope for.

Walking back to her office, Reyes says this is one of the parts that still feels clean. "A person is less free than they should be. You make an argument. They become more free. The logic is direct."

She will spend the next two hours returning phone calls, reviewing evidence in the robbery case, and drafting a suppression motion arguing that her client's traffic stop — and the subsequent search that produced the prosecution's main evidence — was constitutionally defective. It is detailed, careful work, and she does it in a shared office with two other defenders at adjacent desks, phones occasionally ringing, someone's police scanner murmuring in the background.

---

At six o'clock she is still at her desk. The file towers have been disturbed and reconstituted in slightly different shapes.

She is asked whether she thinks the system is broken.

"Broken implies it worked once and something went wrong," she says. "I think it was built with this baked in — the idea that poor defendants would get just enough representation to satisfy the constitutional requirement. Not enough to actually equalize anything." She clicks off one of her monitors. "My job is to fight inside that. I don't know if that makes me complicit or useful. Most days I decide it makes me useful and move on, because the alternative is paralysis and my clients can't afford my paralysis."

She picks up the notebook with the names of her incarcerated clients and puts it in her bag, the way another person might take home a novel they haven't finished.

Tomorrow her first hearing is at eight-thirty.

# The Man Who Knows Everyone

**At Jefferson High, the custodian has outlasted eight principals, four building renovations, and the memory of nearly every teacher on staff. He also knows your kid's name.**

---

Robert "Bobby" Okafor arrives at Jefferson High School before the sun does.

By 5:47 on a Tuesday morning in October, he has already unlocked the east wing, checked the boiler, pressed a mop through the gymnasium hallway, and propped open the back door to air out the locker rooms — a decision made not from any checklist but from 28 years of knowing exactly what the building needs before the building knows it itself.

He is 54 years old. He has worked here since he was 26, when the plan was to do it for one year while he figured out something else. He never exactly figured out something else, and now he is not exactly looking.

"I know where every leak is going to come from when it rains," he says, wrapping both hands around a coffee mug at a folding table in the facilities room — a narrow space behind the boiler that holds a mini-fridge, a corkboard pinned with maintenance schedules, and a small framed photograph of his daughter's college graduation. "I know which radiator in the English hallway starts knocking at 6:15. I know which bathroom door is going to stick when the humidity drops." He pauses. "I know this building the way you know a person you've lived with a long time."

He means that affectionately.

---

Jefferson is a public high school of roughly 1,400 students in a mid-sized city that has seen its share of turbulence — factory closings, demographic shifts, budget cuts that came in waves and left marks. The school has had eight principals since Okafor started. He remembers each one with the measured diplomacy of a man who has learned not to form strong opinions about people who leave.

"Some were good. Some were — " he tilts his hand side to side. "They all had different ideas about how things should run. I just kept the floors clean."

That undersells it. Okafor is, by any reasonable accounting, the institutional memory of Jefferson High. Teachers who have been in the building for fifteen years will walk down to the facilities room with a question — about a pipe, about a room that was used to store something, about whether the auditorium used to have different seats — and find that he already knows the answer and has a follow-up question of his own. A biology teacher named Ms. Gunderson, who has worked at Jefferson for eleven years, says she once asked Okafor where the school kept its extra projector carts. He not only knew where they were but remembered why they'd been moved there, who had moved them, and approximately what year. "He's like a library," she says. "Except he's also the one keeping the library from flooding."

---

The thing people most often mention about Bobby Okafor — the thing that gets brought up in the parking lot, in the main office, in the small biographical entry someone once wrote about him in the school newsletter that he read with visible discomfort — is that he knows the students. Not just the ones who have been here four years. Not just the ones who are loud or in trouble or particularly memorable. He knows them all.

All 1,400 of them.

By name.

Not approximately. Not "the tall one in the blue jacket." By first name, sometimes by nickname, sometimes by last name with a level of specificity that suggests he also knows their older sibling who graduated years ago.

He cannot entirely explain how he does this. He says it started as a personal challenge early in his career and then became a reflex. "When a kid comes in this building, I want them to feel like somebody sees them," he says, without drama, the way he might explain why he stocks extra paper towels near the art rooms. "That's all it is."

What it looks like in practice: a sophomore named Marcus, walking in on a Monday with his hood up and his shoulders in a posture that communicates he would like to be invisible, hears his name said quietly and warmly from the direction of a man pushing a utility cart. Marcus does not fully smile, but something in him adjusts. He says "Hey, Mr. O." He keeps walking, but differently than before.

Okafor watches him go and returns to his cart. "That kid had a rough week last week," he says. "I don't know what. I don't ask. But you can see it."

He sees a lot. He is in the hallways before school, during passing periods, after dismissal, and frequently on weekday evenings when there are games or concerts or meetings. He is the person who finds the crying student in the back stairwell and sits down on the step next to them. He is the person who notices when a kid who usually says good morning stops saying good morning. He is not a counselor. He has no training in this beyond the training of sustained human attention over nearly three decades.

"I've called a counselor more than once," he says, matter-of-factly. "I don't try to be what I'm not. But I can see when something's wrong, and I can make sure somebody who knows more than me finds out about it." He considers this. "Sometimes I'm just the first person to notice. That matters."

Several former students have, over the years, come back to say something like this to him directly. A young woman who graduated six years ago and now teaches elementary school on the other side of the city has said in interviews that Okafor was the first adult at Jefferson who ever knew her name, that she used to walk past him in the morning specifically to hear it. "It sounds small," she said. "It wasn't small."

---

His pay has not kept pace with the cost of living in the city. This is true of most custodians in the district, and Okafor mentions it with neither self-pity nor particular bitterness — more as a structural fact, the way he might note that the east wing roof is older than it should be. He has a union. He has benefits. His daughter graduated from a state school with a degree in nursing. He drives a ten-year-old truck that he maintains himself.

He was offered a supervisory position in the district eight years ago, which would have meant a pay increase and an office and oversight of custodial staff across multiple buildings. He turned it down. When asked why, he is quiet for a moment.

"I would have been a manager," he finally says. "I didn't want to manage. I wanted to be here." He gestures vaguely at the corridor outside the facilities room door, where, at this point in the morning, students have begun arriving in clusters, loud and complicated and carrying all the weight of being seventeen. "This is what I do. I keep this place running. I keep these kids — " He stops, seems slightly embarrassed by the direction the sentence is heading. "I just keep doing what I'm doing."

---

At 7:22 a.m., a freshman girl pauses outside the facilities room door and knocks on the frame. She has a look that suggests she is both embarrassed and in genuine need, which is a common expression among freshmen. She tells Mr. Okafor that someone spilled an entire water bottle in the hallway outside Room 114 and she's worried somebody will slip. 

He is already standing up.

"What's your name?" he asks her, pulling a mop from the corner.

She tells him.

He nods. "I'll know it next time I see you," he says, and heads out the door.

She watches him go with an expression she probably couldn't name. Recognized, maybe. Seen.

He is down the hallway and around the corner before she turns to walk to class.

## What Is a Food Recall?

A food recall is an official action taken by a food manufacturer or a government agency (like the FDA or USDA) to remove a product from the market. This happens when there is a reasonable belief that the food is **mislabeled** (e.g., contains an undeclared allergen like peanuts or milk) or, more seriously, **contaminated** with something that could make people sick, such as harmful bacteria (like *Salmonella* or *Listeria*), foreign objects (like plastic or metal), or toxins.

Think of it as a safety alert. A recall is a precaution to prevent illness. It doesn't necessarily mean everyone who ate the product will get sick, but that there is an identified risk.

### What To Do If You Have a Recalled Product

**1. Don't Panic, But Do Act.**
Recalls are precautionary. Your first step is to confirm you have the *exact* product. Locate it and check the details against the recall notice:
*   **Brand Name & Product Description** (e.g., "ABC Brand Frozen Chopped Spinach")
*   **UPC Code** (the numerical barcode)
*   **Lot/Code Numbers & Dates** (found on the packaging, often near "Best By," "Use By," or on a side seal)
*   **Establishment Number** (for meat/poultry, often inside the USDA mark of inspection)

**2. Do NOT Eat the Product.**
If the details match, stop using it immediately. Do not taste, cook, or feed it to pets.

**3. Decide: Dispose or Return.**
*   **Dispose Safely:** For most recalled items, the recommendation is to throw the product away. To prevent anyone from retrieving it, place it in a sealed bag in an outdoor trash can. If the recall is due to a dangerous contaminant, you may be advised to clean your hands and any surfaces the product touched.
*   **Return for a Refund:** Some retailers or manufacturers will offer a refund. Check the recall notice for instructions. You may need to bring the product (or just a photo of the UPC/lot code) to the store where you bought it.

**4. Clean Up.**
If the recalled product was in your refrigerator or pantry and there's a risk of cross-contamination (common with bacteria like *Listeria*), clean the shelves or drawers where it was stored with hot, soapy water. This is especially important if the recalled item was leaking.

**5. Be Aware of Symptoms.**
If you have already consumed the product, monitor your health. Symptoms of foodborne illness can include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, and fever. If you become ill, contact your healthcare provider and mention the recalled product.

### How to Stay Informed
*   **Check Your Kitchen:** Make a habit of glancing at recall notices from reputable sources.
*   **Register Appliances:** Consider registering your major appliances (like refrigerators) with the manufacturer. They sometimes use this list to notify owners of recalls for products stored inside.
*   **Follow Reputable Sources:** You can find current recall information on websites like:
    *   **FDA Recalls, Market Withdrawals & Safety Alerts**
    *   **USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS) Recalls**
    *   **FoodSafety.gov** (a central hub for U.S. recalls)

**The Bottom Line:** A food recall is a public safety measure. If you have the product, the safest choice is to not consume it and to dispose of it properly. Staying informed helps you protect your household quickly and effectively.

## How Heat Pumps Work & Why You Might Choose One

### The Simple Idea: Moving Heat, Not Making It
Think of a heat pump as a reversible air conditioner. In the summer, it works like a standard AC: it **extracts heat from inside your home and releases it outside**, leaving you cool.

Its magic happens in winter. It reverses the process, **extracting heat from the outside air (even when it's cold) and "pumping" it inside** to warm your home. It does this using a refrigerant that circulates between an outdoor unit and an indoor unit, absorbing and releasing heat through compression and expansion cycles.

### Heat Pump vs. Gas Furnace: Key Differences
*   **Heat Source:** A **heat pump moves existing heat** from one place to another. A **gas furnace creates heat** by burning natural gas (or propane) in a combustion chamber.
*   **Efficiency:** This is the biggest difference. Heat pumps are extremely efficient because they transfer heat rather than generate it. For every unit of electricity they use, they can move **2-4 units of heat energy** into your home (200-400% efficiency). The most efficient gas furnaces convert about 95-98% of the gas's energy into heat.
*   **Function:** Most modern heat pumps provide both **heating and cooling** in one system. A gas furnace only provides heat, requiring a separate air conditioner for summer cooling.

### Why Choose a Heat Pump?
1.  **Lower Operating Costs:** Due to their high efficiency, heat pumps can significantly reduce your heating bills compared to electric furnaces or baseboard heaters. Compared to a gas furnace, the savings depend on your local electricity and gas prices, but they are often competitive and more stable over time.
2.  **All-in-One Comfort:** One system handles both your heating and cooling needs, simplifying maintenance and potentially saving on installation space.
3.  **Safety & Air Quality:** With no on-site combustion, there's no risk of carbon monoxide leaks from the system itself. It doesn't produce fumes or require a gas line or chimney.
4.  **Environmental Impact:** Heat pumps reduce your home's carbon footprint, especially if your electricity comes from renewable sources. They have no direct greenhouse gas emissions at your home.

### Important Considerations
*   **Climate:** Modern cold-climate heat pumps work efficiently in temperatures well below freezing. In regions with prolonged sub-zero (Fahrenheit) temperatures, a supplemental heat source (like electric strips) may be needed for peak cold, which can reduce efficiency advantages.
*   **Upfront Cost:** The installation cost for a heat pump is typically higher than for a standard gas furnace, though comparable to installing both a new furnace *and* a new air conditioner.
*   **Existing Infrastructure:** If you already have efficient gas lines and ducts, the economic case may differ. However, heat pumps are an excellent choice for homes without gas service or for those replacing both an old furnace and an old AC unit.

**In short:** A heat pump is an efficient, all-electric system that provides year-round comfort by moving heat. It's a compelling choice for lower energy bills, simplified HVAC, and reducing reliance on fossil fuels, particularly in mild to cold climates (with the right model) and for homes seeking an integrated heating and cooling solution.

### What Are Municipal Bonds (and Why Do Cities Use Them)?

Think of a municipal bond like a loan you give to your city or local government. When you buy one, you're lending your money to help pay for things like fixing roads, building schools, or improving parks. In return, the city promises to pay you back with interest over time.

**In simple terms:** It's how your town borrows money from the public to fund big projects it can't pay for immediately with its regular tax income.

### Why Do Cities Issue These Bonds?

Cities issue bonds for the same reason many people take out a mortgage: they need to make a large, important purchase now, but will pay for it over many years. Here’s why this makes sense for communities:

1.  **To Build and Maintain Big Public Projects.** A city's annual budget from taxes and fees often isn't enough to pay for a new library, water treatment plant, or hospital all at once. Bonds let them raise the full amount upfront and complete the project, benefiting the community immediately.

2.  **To Spread the Cost Over Time.** The projects funded by bonds—like a new highway or school—are used by residents for decades. Bonds allow the cost to be paid back over that same long period, so current and future taxpayers who use the project help share the expense.
